data.title: Knee Pain Differential: Structural vs Inflammatory vs Referred
data.seoTitle: Knee Pain Differential: Structural vs Inflammatory vs Referred | NP C…
data.seoDescription: Advanced NP clinical review: Knee Pain Differential: Structural vs Inflammatory vs Referred. Clinical reasoning and management for NP primary care practice.

sections.heading: Clinical Framing
sections.body: Knee pain is among the top 10 outpatient musculoskeletal complaints NPs manage. The central challenge is separating benign, self-limited structural injuries from inflammatory arthropathies and referred pain, which require distinct management pathways. Missing septic arthritis or an acute ligamentous tear requiring surgical consultation is a high-stakes error in primary care.

sections.heading: Core Concepts & Differential
Management Approach
Red Flags & Escalation
sections.body:
[
  "Fever + monoarthritis → septic arthritis until proven otherwise → JOINT ASPIRATION or ED",
  "Inability to bear weight + suspected fracture → Ottawa rules positive → X-ray, then orthopedic if fracture",
  "Acute locked knee (unable to extend) → acute meniscal bucket-handle → orthopedic same-day/next-day referral",
  "Rapidly expanding effusion, warmth disproportionate to trauma → consider hemarthrosis or infection",
  "Suspected ACL/PCL complete tear with significant instability → orthopedic referral within 1-2 weeks",
  "Suspected malignancy: night pain, bone tenderness, systemic symptoms in child/young adult → urgent imaging"
]

data.title: UTI Management: Uncomplicated vs Complicated, Antibiotic Stewardship
data.seoTitle: UTI Management: Uncomplicated vs Complicated, Antibiotic Stewardship …
data.seoDescription: Advanced NP clinical review: UTI Management: Uncomplicated vs Complicated, Antibiotic Stewardship. Clinical reasoning and management for NP primary care practi…

sections.heading: Clinical Framing
sections.body: UTI is the most common reason antibiotics are prescribed in outpatient settings. NPs must distinguish uncomplicated from complicated UTI to select the safest, most effective antibiotic and avoid contributing to resistance. Inappropriate fluoroquinolone prescribing for uncomplicated UTI is a major stewardship failure. Missed pyelonephritis and missed UTI in pregnancy carry serious consequences.

sections.heading: Core Concepts & Differential
Management Approach
sections.body:
{
  "diagnosis": [
    "Urinalysis with microscopy: pyuria (≥5 WBC/hpf), nitrites (gram-negative bacteria), leukocyte esterase; dipstick has ~80% sensitivity for pyuria",
    "Urine culture: indicated for complicated UTI, recurrent UTI, pyelonephritis, pregnancy, treatment failure; not required for straightforward uncomplicated cystitis in healthy women",
    "Clinical diagnosis without culture is acceptable for uncomplicated cystitis if symptoms are classic"
  ],
  "antibioticSelection": {
    "uncomplicatedCystitis_firstLine": [
      "Nitrofurantoin monohydrate/macrocrystals (Macrobid): 100 mg BID x 5 days — preferred due to minimal resistance and low collateral damage. AVOID if CrCl <30 mL/min or pyelonephritis (poor tissue penetration).",
      "Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX): 160/800 mg (DS) BID x 3 days — use only if local resistance <20%. Avoid in first trimester and at term.",
      "Fosfomycin: 3 g sachet single dose — convenient, good for E. coli; less effective against Klebsiella."
    ],
    "avoid_uncomplicated": "Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin): AVOID for uncomplicated cystitis — high collateral damage, tendinopathy/aortic aneurysm risk, reserve for complicated UTI and pyelonephritis when other agents unsuitable.",
    "complicatedUTI_pyelonephritis_outpatient": [
      "Ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID x 7 days (if local susceptibility adequate, avoid if community fluoroquinolone resistance >10%)",
      "TMP-SMX DS BID x 14 days (culture-guided)",
      "Oral cephalosporin (cephalexin 500 mg QID x 7-10 days) — less preferred for pyelonephritis but adequate for complicated cystitis",
      "Obtain culture BEFORE starting antibiotics for all complicated UTI; adjust based on susceptibilities"
    ],
    "maleSex": "Consider 7-10 day course; exclude prostatitis (perineal pain, tender boggy prostate on DRE). If prostatitis suspected: 4-6 weeks fluoroquinolone or TMP-SMX (excellent prostate penetration).",
    "catheterAssociated": "Treat only if symptomatic (CAUTI). Remove or replace catheter if possible. Culture-directed therapy 5-7 days."
  },
  "pregnancy": {
    "screening": "Screen all pregnant patients for asymptomatic bacteriuria at first prenatal visit; treat if positive (reduces pyelonephritis, preterm labor).",
    "safeAntibiotics": [
      "Nitrofurantoin: safe in 2nd trimester; avoid at 38+ weeks (theoretical hemolytic anemia in neonate)",
      "Cephalexin: safe throughout pregnancy",
      "Amoxicillin-clavulanate: acceptable but higher resistance"
    ],
    "avoid_pregnancy": [
      "TMP-SMX (first trimester — folate antagonist; near term — kernicterus risk)",
      "Fluoroquinolones (theoretical cartilage risk)"
    ],
    "pyelonephritis_pregnancy": "Hospitalize in most cases due to risk of preterm labor and sepsis"
  },
  "recurrentUTI": {
    "definition": "≥2 episodes in 6 months or ≥3 in 12 months",
    "workup": "Urine culture, consider urology referral to rule out structural abnormality",
    "prevention": [
      "Behavioral: post-coital voiding, front-to-back hygiene, avoid spermicides",
      "Vaginal estrogen cream for postmenopausal women — reduces recurrence significantly",
      "Post-coital prophylaxis (single dose TMP-SMX or nitrofurantoin) if coitally-related",
      "Continuous low-dose prophylaxis (nitrofurantoin 50-100 mg nightly x 6-12 months) for frequent recurrence",
      "Cranberry products: modest evidence, safe adjunct"
    ]
  }
}

sections.heading: Red Flags & Escalation
sections.body:
[
  "Fever + flank pain + nausea → pyelonephritis → obtain culture, treat with fluoroquinolone or cephalosporin, admit if cannot tolerate orals",
  "Sepsis signs (HR >100, BP <90, altered mentation) → urosepsis → ED IMMEDIATELY",
  "UTI in pregnancy with systemic symptoms → hospitalize",
  "Treatment failure at 72 hours → culture and susceptibilities essential → escalate antibiotic",
  "Recurrent UTI in males → prostate workup, urology referral"
]

data.title: Type 2 Diabetes: Intensification Beyond Metformin
data.seoTitle: Type 2 Diabetes: Intensification Beyond Metformin | NP Clinical Review
data.seoDescription: Advanced NP clinical review: Type 2 Diabetes: Intensification Beyond Metformin. Advanced prescribing, management, and decision-making for NP boards.

sections.heading: Clinical Framing
sections.body: Metformin remains the foundational agent for T2DM, but >50% of patients require intensification within 3 years. The NP's challenge at intensification is not just picking an antihyperglycemic — it is selecting the agent that simultaneously treats the most important comorbidity risk. GLP-1 receptor agonists and SGLT-2 inhibitors have transformed T2DM management by offering cardiovascular and renal protection independent of A1c lowering. Knowing when and how to prescribe these agents, including dosing, monitoring, and contraindications, is now core NP competency.

sections.heading: Core Concepts & Differential
sections.body:
{
  "whenToIntensify": [
    "A1c not at individualized goal after 3 months of maximum tolerated metformin",
    "At diagnosis: if A1c ≥10% or patient is symptomatic (polyuria, weight loss) → start dual therapy or insulin initially",
    "Progressive beta-cell failure over time despite prior adherence"
  ],
  "individualizedA1cTargets": {
    "general_healthy": "<7.0% (ADA 2024)",
    "older_adult_healthy_longExpectancy": "<7.5%",
    "older_adult_complex_comorbidities": "7.5-8.0% — tighter control risks hypoglycemia, falls",
    "older_adult_veryComplex_limitedExpectancy": "<8.5% — avoid hypoglycemia as primary goal"
  },
  "drugClasses": {
    "GLP1_receptorAgonists": {
      "examples": "Semaglutide (Ozempic SC, Rybelsus oral), liraglutide (Victoza), dulaglutide (Trulicity)",
      "mechanism": "Incretin mimetic: glucose-dependent insulin secretion, glucagon suppression, delayed gastric emptying, satiety",
      "keyBenefits": [
        "Cardiovascular: MACE reduction in ASCVD-proven patients (LEADER, SUSTAIN-6)",
        "Weight loss (average 5-10 lbs, semaglutide up to 15 lbs)",
        "Low intrinsic hypoglycemia risk"
      ],
      "semaglutide_dosing": "Start 0.25 mg SC weekly x 4 weeks → 0.5 mg weekly → titrate to 1 mg then 2 mg at 4-week intervals based on response/tolerability",
      "semaglutide_oral": "3 mg daily x 1 month → 7 mg → 14 mg; must take on empty stomach with ≤120 mL water; NO food for 30 min after",
      "contraindications": [
        "Personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma",
        "Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2 (MEN2)",
        "Prior pancreatitis (relative — use with caution)"
      ],
      "sideEffects": "Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea (especially initial titration — most resolve); rare: pancreatitis, gallbladder disease",
      "monitoring": "A1c q3 months until stable; weight; GI symptoms; renal function baseline"
    },
    "SGLT2_inhibitors": {
      "examples": "Empagliflozin (Jardiance), dapagliflozin (Farxiga), canagliflozin (Invokana)",
      "mechanism": "Block renal glucose reabsorption → glycosuria; also reduces sodium reabsorption → blood pressure, weight loss, volume reduction",
      "keyBenefits": [
        "Heart failure: reduced HHF (EMPEROR-Reduced, DAPA-HF) — beneficial even without T2DM",
        "CKD: slows GFR decline (CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD)",
        "ASCVD: modest CV benefit (EMPA-REG)",
        "Weight loss 2-4 kg",
        "BP reduction 2-4 mmHg systolic"
      ],
      "empagliflozin_dosing": "10 mg daily (start); may increase to 25 mg daily",
      "dapagliflozin_dosing": "10 mg daily (approved for HFrEF and CKD even without T2DM)",
      "contraindications": [
        "eGFR <30 (no glycemic effect; still CKD-protective role for dapagliflozin/empagliflozin down to eGFR ≥20 for HF/CKD indication)",
        "Type 1 DM (DKA risk)",
        "Recurrent UTIs or genital mycotic infections (relative)"
      ],
      "sideEffects": "Genital mycotic infections (most common — counsel on hygiene), UTI, polyuria; rare: DKA (euglycemic — measure ketones not just glucose if symptomatic), Fournier's gangrene, volume depletion",
      "hold_perioperative": "Hold SGLT-2 inhibitors 3 days before major surgery (euglycemic DKA risk)",
      "monitoring": "eGFR and potassium q6 months; genital symptoms; weight"
    },
    "DPP4_inhibitors": {
      "examples": "Sitagliptin (Januvia), saxagliptin, linagliptin",
      "mechanism": "Inhibit DPP-4 enzyme → increased endogenous GLP-1 → glucose-dependent insulin secretion",
      "keyBenefits": [
        "Weight neutral",
        "Low hypoglycemia risk",
        "Renal dose adjustment available (sitagliptin)",
        "CV safety confirmed (TECOS, SAVOR-TIMI)"
      ],
      "linagliptin_advantage": "No renal dose adjustment required — preferred in CKD across all stages",
      "saxagliptin_caution": "Increased HHF risk — AVOID in heart failure",
      "dosing": "Sitagliptin 100 mg daily (reduce to 50 mg if CrCl 30-50; 25 mg if <30); Linagliptin 5 mg daily",
      "modest_A1c_lowering": "0.5-0.8% — less potent than GLP-1 or SGLT-2"
    },
    "sulfonylureas": {
      "examples": "Glipizide, glimepiride, glyburide",
      "mechanism": "Stimulate beta-cell insulin secretion independent of glucose",
      "keyBenefits": [
        "Inexpensive",
        "Significant A1c lowering (1-2%)",
        "Oral"
      ],
      "majorRisk": "HYPOGLYCEMIA — especially in elderly, CKD, missed meals",
      "doNotUse": "Glyburide in older adults (long-acting, active metabolites accumulate) — Beers Criteria",
      "preferred": "Glipizide (shorter-acting, less hypoglycemia) if sulfonylurea needed",
      "weightGain": "2-4 kg typical; not preferred in obese patients"
    },
    "insulin": {
      "when": "A1c >10%, symptomatic hyperglycemia, failure of oral agents, acute illness, pregnancy",
      "initiation": "Basal insulin (glargine, detemir) starting at 10 units SC nightly (or 0.1-0.2 units/kg); titrate by 2 units every 3 days to fasting glucose 80-130",
      "olderAdult_caution": "Avoid complex regimens; use prefilled pens; titrate slowly; consider CGM to detect nocturnal hypoglycemia"
    }
  },
  "comorbidityDrivenSelection": {
    "ASCVD_confirmed": "Add GLP-1 RA (semaglutide, liraglutide) or SGLT-2 inhibitor (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) — both reduce MACE",
    "HFrEF": "SGLT-2 inhibitor first (dapagliflozin or empagliflozin) — strongest HHF evidence; AVOID saxagliptin (DPP-4), thiazolidinediones",
    "CKD_stage3b4": "SGLT-2 inhibitor for nephroprotection; GLP-1 RA safe; avoid metformin if eGFR <30; avoid sulfonylureas",
    "weightLoss_needed": "GLP-1 RA > SGLT-2 inhibitor > DPP-4 > sulfonylureas/insulin",
    "costConstrained": "Sulfonylureas or NPH insulin as least expensive options; generic metformin+glipizide combination"
  }
}

sections.heading: Management Approach
sections.body:
{
  "stepwiseIntensification": [
    "Step 1: Optimize metformin (max tolerated dose with food); add GI-protective strategies",
    "Step 2: If A1c not at goal after 3 months — add second agent based on comorbidity driver (ASCVD → GLP-1 or SGLT-2; HF → SGLT-2; CKD → SGLT-2; weight → GLP-1; cost → sulfonylurea)",
    "Step 3: Triple therapy if needed — GLP-1 + SGLT-2 + metformin is well-tolerated combination with complementary mechanisms",
    "Step 4: Add basal insulin if oral/injectable non-insulin agents inadequate"
  ],
  "prescribingWorkflow_GLP1": [
    "Check contraindications (thyroid cancer personal/family history, MEN2, pancreatitis history)",
    "Start lowest dose; titrate monthly",
    "Counsel on GI side effects (nausea resolves in 4-8 weeks for most); take with small meal",
    "Ensure patient has auto-injector training or practice in office",
    "Monitor weight, A1c at 3 months"
  ]
}

sections.heading: Red Flags & Escalation
sections.body:
[
  "Euglycemic DKA on SGLT-2 inhibitor: normal or mildly elevated glucose, but ketonemia — check serum/urine ketones if abdominal pain, nausea, fatigue; hold SGLT-2 and send to ED",
  "Severe hypoglycemia on sulfonylurea or insulin: blood glucose <54 mg/dL with symptoms — oral glucose if conscious; glucagon kit if unconscious; reduce sulfonylurea dose",
  "Pancreatitis symptoms (persistent severe epigastric pain radiating to back) on GLP-1 RA → hold medication → ED evaluation",
  "Rapid A1c decline from >10% → do NOT target <7% immediately (increased risk of MACE events) — gradual reduction over 3-6 months"
]

sections.heading: Population Considerations
Follow-Up & Monitoring
sections.body:
{
  "schedule": "A1c every 3 months until at goal, then every 6 months; BP at every visit; weight at every visit",
  "labs": "BMP (renal function, potassium) every 6-12 months; annual lipid panel; annual urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (UACR); LFTs if on thiazolidinediones",
  "complications_surveillance": "Annual dilated fundus exam; annual foot exam with monofilament; dental referral; depression screening (PHQ-9)",
  "selfMonitoring": "SMBG most useful for those on insulin or sulfonylureas; CGM preferred if on insulin; discuss hypoglycemia recognition and treatment plan",
  "referral_triggers": "Nephrology if eGFR <30 or rapidly declining; ophthalmology for any retinopathy; endocrinology if A1c uncontrolled despite triple therapy or complex insulin needs"
}

data.title: COPD: GOLD Staging, Inhaler Selection, and Exacerbation Prevention
data.seoTitle: COPD: GOLD Staging, Inhaler Selection, and Exacerbation Prevention | …
data.seoDescription: Advanced NP clinical review: COPD: GOLD Staging, Inhaler Selection, and Exacerbation Prevention. Advanced prescribing, management, and decision-making for NP b…

sections.heading: Clinical Framing
sections.body: COPD is the third leading cause of death in the US and is profoundly under-treated in primary care. NPs frequently manage COPD longitudinally, including initial spirometric diagnosis, GOLD-based inhaler escalation, exacerbation management, and admission triage. The most common errors are: under-diagnosing COPD, under-treating with only PRN SABA, over-relying on ICS without clear indication, and delaying pulmonary rehabilitation referral.

sections.heading: Core Concepts & Differential
Management Approach
sections.body:
{
  "exacerbation_inPrimaryCare": {
    "definition": "Acute worsening of respiratory symptoms beyond normal day-to-day variation requiring change in medication",
    "antibiotics": [
      "Indicated when: change in sputum color AND increased dyspnea AND increased sputum volume (Anthonisen criteria all 3, or 2 of 3 in moderate-severe exacerbation)",
      "Azithromycin 500 mg day 1, then 250 mg x 4 days — first-line (covers atypicals + H. influenzae)",
      "Doxycycline 100 mg BID x 5 days — alternative",
      "Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg BID x 5-7 days — if Moraxella or beta-lactamase concern",
      "Fluoroquinolones: reserve for severe exacerbation with risk factors for P. aeruginosa (frequent hospitalizations, prior Pseudomonas culture, severe COPD)"
    ],
    "systemicSteroids": "Prednisone 40 mg daily x 5 days — reduces treatment failure and duration of hospitalization; NO benefit to longer courses",
    "SABA_escalation": "Increase frequency of SABA MDI (albuterol 2-4 puffs q4h); add ipratropium if not already on SAMA",
    "oxygen": "Maintain SpO2 88-92% in known CO2 retainers — avoid driving SpO2 >95% (blunts hypoxic drive)"
  },
  "admissionCriteria": [
    "Severe dyspnea not responding to initial bronchodilators",
    "SpO2 <88% on room air or acute respiratory failure",
    "Altered mental status",
    "Failure of outpatient treatment within 48-72 hours",
    "Significant comorbidities (pneumonia, cardiac arrhythmia, heart failure)",
    "Poor social support or inability to manage at home"
  ],
  "nonPharmacologic": [
    "Pulmonary rehabilitation: strongest evidence for improved exercise tolerance and quality of life; refer all GOLD 2-4 patients",
    "Smoking cessation: single most important intervention — slows FEV1 decline",
    "Vaccinations: annual influenza, RSV (adults ≥60), pneumococcal (PPSV23 and/or PCV20)",
    "Oxygen therapy: if PaO2 ≤55 mmHg or SpO2 ≤88% at rest — improves survival; prescribe for ≥15 hours/day"
  ],
  "inhalerTechnique": "Assess at every visit. Common errors: not shaking MDI, not breath-holding, pressing canister too early. Spacer use improves delivery. DPI (dry powder inhalers): require sufficient inspiratory flow — may be problematic in severe COPD."
}

sections.heading: Red Flags & Escalation
sections.body:
[
  "SpO2 <88% at rest → supplemental oxygen + ED evaluation",
  "Acute-on-chronic respiratory failure (PCO2 rising, pH falling) → ED/hospital",
  "Failure to respond to antibiotics + steroids at 72 hours → escalate care",
  "New hemoptysis + weight loss → consider malignancy → CT chest",
  "First exacerbation in a previously stable patient → re-evaluate diagnosis (rule out pneumonia, pneumothorax, PE, cardiac cause)",
  "Bullae or pneumothorax on imaging → avoid high-flow oxygen; thoracic surgery consultation"
]

sections.heading: Population Considerations

data.title: Headache Differential: Benign vs Emergent Red Flags
data.seoTitle: Headache Differential: Benign vs Emergent Red Flags | NP Clinical Rev…
data.seoDescription: Advanced NP clinical review: Headache Differential: Benign vs Emergent Red Flags. Advanced prescribing, management, and decision-making for NP boards.

sections.heading: Clinical Framing
sections.body: Headache is one of the most common NP presentations — yet missing a subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH), meningitis, or hypertensive emergency is catastrophic. The primary care NP's job is not to diagnose every secondary headache type but to reliably identify who is safe to manage outpatient and who requires immediate escalation. The SNOOP4 framework is essential and must become reflex.

sections.heading: Management Approach
Red Flags & Escalation
sections.body:
[
  "Thunderclap → ED immediately → CT head + LP if CT negative",
  "Fever + headache + stiff neck → meningitis → ED, blood cultures, empiric antibiotics without delay",
  "Age >50 + new unilateral temporal headache → GCA → start steroids SAME DAY before vision lost",
  "Headache + progressive neuro symptoms → mass/ICP → urgent CT head",
  "Headache + rash (especially petechial) → meningococcemia → 911 / ED immediately",
  "Postcoital, exertional, or cough-related thunderclap → SAH until proven otherwise"
]

sections.heading: Population Considerations
Follow-Up & Monitoring

data.title: Syncope Differential: Vasovagal vs Cardiac vs Neurological
data.seoTitle: Syncope Differential: Vasovagal vs Cardiac vs Neurological | NP Clini…
data.seoDescription: Advanced NP clinical review: Syncope Differential: Vasovagal vs Cardiac vs Neurological. Advanced prescribing, management, and decision-making for NP boards.

sections.heading: Clinical Framing
sections.body: Syncope causes 1-3% of ED visits and ~6% of hospital admissions. The central challenge for NPs: roughly 15% of syncope has a cardiac etiology with significantly higher 1-year mortality. Missing structural heart disease or a serious arrhythmia in a patient who 'just fainted' is a preventable catastrophe. Vasovagal syncope — the most common cause — is a diagnosis of exclusion established by pattern, not by absence of workup.

sections.heading: Core Concepts & Differential
Management Approach
sections.body:
{
  "mandatoryInitialWorkup": [
    "12-lead ECG — mandatory for ALL patients with syncope",
    "Orthostatic vital signs",
    "Detailed history: duration, prodrome, position, activity at onset, recovery time, witnesses account, prior episodes, medications",
    "Blood glucose check",
    "Pregnancy test in women of reproductive age with syncope"
  ],
  "additionalWorkup_based_onRisk": [
    "Echocardiogram: if structural heart disease suspected (murmur, exertional syncope, abnormal ECG)",
    "Holter monitor or event recorder: for recurrent unexplained syncope — arrhythmia detection",
    "Tilt table testing: for recurrent vasovagal when diagnosis uncertain",
    "Troponin: if cardiac ischemia possible",
    "Neuroimaging (CT/MRI): NOT routinely indicated — only if focal neuro deficit, head trauma during fall, or atypical presentation suggesting seizure"
  ],
  "vasovagal_management": [
    "Education and reassurance",
    "Counterpressure maneuvers during prodrome (leg crossing, arm tensing, squatting) — proven to abort many episodes",
    "Increase fluid and salt intake (if not hypertensive or cardiac compromised)",
    "Avoid triggers (prolonged standing, hot environments, dehydration)",
    "Fludrocortisone: 0.1 mg daily — second-line for recurrent vasovagal with volume depletion",
    "Midodrine: 5-10 mg TID — alpha-agonist; increases venous tone; useful in orthostatic hypotension"
  ]
}

sections.heading: Red Flags & Escalation
sections.body:
[
  "Exertional syncope → structural heart disease until proven otherwise → ECG + ECHO + cardiology same-day to next-day",
  "Syncope with abnormal ECG (QTc >500, Brugada, WPW) → cardiology or ED",
  "Syncope without prodrome in older adult → high arrhythmic suspicion → hospital admission",
  "Syncope with chest pain or dyspnea → ACS or PE → ED",
  "Syncope with heart failure signs → ROSE/SF criteria → admit",
  "Family history of sudden cardiac death + young patient with exertional syncope → HOCM workup emergently"
]

sections.heading: Population Considerations
Follow-Up & Monitoring

data.title: Red Flags in Hypertension: When to Refer or Admit
data.seoTitle: Red Flags in Hypertension: When to Refer or Admit | NP Clinical Review
data.seoDescription: Advanced NP clinical review: Red Flags in Hypertension: When to Refer or Admit. Clinical reasoning and management for NP primary care practice.

sections.heading: Clinical Framing
sections.body: Most hypertension is asymptomatic and managed longitudinally. However, the NP must reliably distinguish hypertensive urgency from emergency — and within emergency, identify which organ is acutely damaged to direct immediate ED transfer. The critical error to avoid: rapidly lowering BP in urgency (causing ischemic harm) or failing to recognize an emergency and managing as urgency.

sections.heading: Core Concepts & Differential
Management Approach
sections.body:
{
  "urgency_approach": [
    "Do NOT administer rapid-acting oral antihypertensives (sublingual nifedipine is DANGEROUS — precipitates stroke, MI)",
    "Reassess BP after the patient rests in quiet room for 20-30 minutes — white coat effect is real",
    "If still ≥180/120 without end-organ damage: restart medications if patient was non-adherent; adjust existing regimen; add or increase second agent",
    "Arrange follow-up within 24-48 hours",
    "Ensure the patient is not symptomatic in a way that suggests emergency"
  ],
  "emergency_approach": [
    "Transfer to ED IMMEDIATELY — do not treat empirically in outpatient setting with IV agents",
    "Call 911 if patient is hemodynamically unstable, seizing, or severely altered",
    "Document findings clearly: BP, symptoms, neuro exam, and rationale for transfer"
  ]
}

sections.heading: Red Flags & Escalation
sections.body:
[
  "Any neurological symptom + markedly elevated BP → stroke vs hypertensive encephalopathy → ED",
  "Tearing chest/back pain → aortic dissection → ED + call 911",
  "Acute pulmonary edema (dyspnea, orthopnea, crackles) → hypertensive emergency with HF → ED",
  "Visual changes (blurring, scotoma) + severely elevated BP → hypertensive retinopathy or PRES → ED with ophthalmology",
  "Pregnancy + BP ≥160/110 + any symptom → preeclampsia/eclampsia → obstetric emergency"
]

sections.heading: Population Considerations
Follow-Up & Monitoring

data.title: Cancer Red Flags in Primary Care: When to Expedite Workup
data.seoTitle: Cancer Red Flags in Primary Care: When to Expedite Workup | NP Clinic…
data.seoDescription: Advanced NP clinical review: Cancer Red Flags in Primary Care: When to Expedite Workup. Clinical reasoning and management for NP primary care practice.

sections.heading: Clinical Framing
sections.body: NPs in primary care are the first to detect cancer. The challenge: cancer symptoms are often insidious and nonspecific. The risk of under-investigation must be balanced against anxiety-inducing over-investigation. Knowing which symptom combinations warrant expedited workup versus watchful waiting is a high-stakes NP judgment call. NICE NG12 cancer referral guidelines provide structure for this decision.

sections.heading: Core Concepts & Differential
Management Approach
Red Flags & Escalation
sections.body:
[
  "Supraclavicular lymphadenopathy → malignancy until proven otherwise → urgent CT + oncology referral",
  "Palpable abdominal mass or hepatomegaly → CT abdomen/pelvis within days",
  "Gross hematuria → urology within 2 weeks regardless of pain status",
  "New adult seizure → MRI brain with contrast → neurology",
  "New bone pain + weight loss + hypercalcemia → metastatic disease → oncology",
  "Progressive jaundice + weight loss + back pain → pancreatic malignancy → CT + GI/surgery"
]

sections.heading: Population Considerations
Follow-Up & Monitoring

data.title: PrEP for HIV Prevention: Eligibility, Prescribing, and Monitoring
data.seoTitle: PrEP for HIV Prevention: Eligibility, Prescribing, and Monitoring | N…
data.seoDescription: Advanced NP clinical review: PrEP for HIV Prevention: Eligibility, Prescribing, and Monitoring. Advanced prescribing, management, and decision-making for NP bo…

sections.heading: Clinical Framing
sections.body: PrEP (pre-exposure prophylaxis) for HIV is an NP-level prescribing competency with profound public health impact. Gay and bisexual men, transgender women, people with injection drug use (PWID), and serodiscordant couples represent the populations with highest PrEP benefit. Effective NP-led PrEP programs require: inclusive assessment language, evidence-based eligibility determination, correct regimen selection, and rigorous monitoring. Stigma and assumptions are the primary barriers to PrEP access — the NP encounter must actively counter both.

sections.heading: Core Concepts & Differential
Management Approach
sections.body:
{
  "baseline_workup": [
    "HIV-1 RNA (4th-generation antigen/antibody test or NAT) — MUST BE NEGATIVE within 1 week of prescribing",
    "Hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg), surface antibody (anti-HBs) — TDF/FTC and TAF/FTC active against HBV; do NOT stop PrEP without plan for HBV management (flare risk)",
    "Hepatitis C antibody",
    "STI: gonorrhea/chlamydia (urethral + rectal + pharyngeal NAAT), syphilis RPR",
    "Creatinine/eGFR",
    "Pregnancy test if applicable"
  ],
  "followUp_monitoring": {
    "HIV_test": "Every 3 months — MANDATORY. If patient tests positive at any follow-up → STOP PrEP immediately → refer to HIV specialist for resistance testing and ART initiation",
    "renalFunction": "Every 6 months (Truvada); annually (Descovy); immediately if symptoms of renal tubular dysfunction",
    "STI_screening": "Every 3-6 months (site-specific NAAT); syphilis RPR every 6 months minimum",
    "hepatitis_C": "Annually for MSM or PWID",
    "adherence_counseling": "At every visit — review missed doses, discuss barriers; PrEP only effective if taken correctly"
  },
  "adherence_counseling": [
    "Discuss 7-day vs 21-day coverage window based on exposure type",
    "Address stigma: some patients prefer not to fill at local pharmacy — mail-order options",
    "Mobile apps, pill organizers as adherence tools",
    "Event-driven (2+1+1) PrEP: not FDA-approved in US but used off-label for MSM with infrequent exposures — 2 pills 2-24h before, 1 pill 24h after, 1 pill 48h after"
  ]
}

sections.heading: Red Flags & Escalation
sections.body:
[
  "HIV-positive result at any monitoring visit → STOP PrEP → urgent HIV specialist referral within days → resistance testing → ART initiation",
  "Significant renal function decline on Truvada → switch to Descovy or injectable cabotegravir",
  "Hepatitis B flare if TDF/FTC discontinued without HBV management plan → hepatology consultation",
  "Worsening STI incidence despite PrEP → intensify behavioral counseling; increase screening frequency; ensure treatment of partners"
]

data.title: Geriatric Assessment: Falls, Polypharmacy, Functional Status, and Frailty
data.seoTitle: Geriatric Assessment: Falls, Polypharmacy, Functional Status, and Fra…
data.seoDescription: Advanced NP clinical review: Geriatric Assessment: Falls, Polypharmacy, Functional Status, and Frailty. Advanced prescribing, management, and decision-making f…

sections.heading: Clinical Framing
sections.body: Comprehensive geriatric assessment (CGA) is the gold standard for NP-led care of complex older adults. Falls cause 20,000 deaths/year in the US; polypharmacy causes more preventable hospitalizations than any single disease; frailty predicts outcomes from surgery to chemo to ICU better than age alone. NPs who conduct even abbreviated CGA detect actionable problems invisible to problem-focused visits.

sections.heading: Core Concepts & Differential
sections.body:
{
  "falls": {
    "epidemiology": "1 in 3 adults >65 fall annually; most do not report falls unless asked. 10% result in serious injury (hip fracture, subdural hematoma).",
    "CDC_STEADI_toolkit": "Standardized falls assessment framework: Ask, Assess, Intervene",
    "ask_about_falls": "ALWAYS ask: 'Have you fallen in the past year?' and 'Do you feel unsteady when standing or walking?'",
    "functional_tests": {
      "TUG_test": "Timed Up and Go: patient rises from chair, walks 10 feet, returns, sits. ≥12 seconds = increased fall risk. Normal <10 seconds.",
      "30secChairStand": "Counts how many times patient can rise from chair in 30 seconds; assesses lower extremity strength",
      "4StageBalance": "Feet together, semi-tandem, tandem, single-leg stance — inability to hold 10 seconds each = fall risk"
    },
    "intrinsicRiskFactors": [
      "Previous fall (strongest predictor)",
      "Gait/balance impairment",
      "Cognitive decline",
      "Lower extremity weakness",
      "Visual impairment",
      "Depression",
      "Postural hypotension"
    ],
    "medicationContributors": {
      "high_risk_classes": [
        "Benzodiazepines (psychomotor slowing, sedation)",
        "Z-drugs (zolpidem — Beers Criteria)",
        "Tricyclic antidepressants (anticholinergic, orthostatic)",
        "First-generation antihistamines (diphenhydramine)",
        "Opioids",
        "Antihypertensives in excess — orthostatic hypotension",
        "Diuretics — dehydration, orthostatic",
        "Antipsychotics (extrapyramidal effects)",
        "Gabapentin/pregabalin — dizziness"
      ],
      "action": "Deprescribe fall-risk medications whenever possible; if essential, lowest effective dose + fall prevention strategies"
    },
    "extrinsicFactors": [
      "Poor lighting",
      "Loose rugs",
      "Clutter on stairs",
      "No grab bars in bathroom",
      "Inappropriate footwear"
    ],
    "intervention": "Multi-component falls prevention: exercise (balance/strength training), medication review, home safety assessment, vision correction, vitamin D supplementation if deficient"
  },
  "polypharmacy": {
    "definition": "Concurrent use of ≥5 medications (some define ≥10 as hyper-polypharmacy)",
    "prevalence": ">50% of adults >65 take ≥5 medications; >10% take ≥10",
    "risks": "Adverse drug reactions, drug-drug interactions, cascade prescribing, non-adherence, cognitive burden, falls, hospitalization",
    "cascadePrescribing": "New medication added to treat a symptom caused by another medication — result is layered iatrogenic harm. Example: NSAID → ankle edema → added amlodipine increased → more edema → added furosemide → electrolyte imbalance → added potassium.",
    "STOPPStart_criteria": "Screening Tool of Older Person's Prescriptions (STOPP) and Screening Tool to Alert to Right Treatment (START) — validated tools for identifying inappropriate and missing medications in older adults",
    "MRPrescribing_NP_role": "Conduct medication reconciliation at every visit; review OTCs, supplements, PRN medications; ask about adherence and cost barriers"
  },
  "frailty": {
    "friedCriteria": {
      "description": "Five component phenotypic model",
      "components": [
        "Unintentional weight loss >4.5 kg (10 lbs) in past year",
        "Self-reported exhaustion (CES-D questionnaire)",
        "Weakness (grip strength below sex/BMI threshold)",
        "Slow gait speed (<0.8 m/s or <6 m in 6 seconds)",
        "Low physical activity (kcal/week below sex threshold)"
      ],
      "scoring": "0 = robust; 1-2 = pre-frail; ≥3 = frail",
      "clinicalImplication": "Frail older adults have 2-4x higher mortality, hospitalization, and ADL disability; frailty predicts surgical outcomes better than age"
    },
    "clinicalFrailtyScale": "9-point scale (1=very fit to 9=terminally ill) used for rapid clinical estimation without formal testing — widely used in acute settings",
    "frailty_intervention": "Resistance exercise is the most evidence-based intervention; address malnutrition; optimize chronic disease management; reduce unnecessary medications"
  },
  "functionalStatus": {
    "ADLs": "Activities of Daily Living: bathing, dressing, grooming, toileting, transferring, continence, feeding — decline indicates need for supportive services",
    "IADLs": "Instrumental ADLs: phone use, shopping, cooking, housekeeping, laundry, transportation, medications, finances — more sensitive to early cognitive decline",
    "assessment_tools": "Barthel Index (ADLs), Lawton Scale (IADLs)",
    "clinicalRule": "New or progressive ADL/IADL decline in an older adult is a red flag — evaluate for acute illness, medication side effect, depression, or cognitive decline before attributing to 'just aging'"
  },
  "cognitiveScreening": {
    "tools": [
      "Mini-Cog (3-word recall + clock draw) — quick 3 min; high sensitivity in primary care",
      "MoCA (Montreal Cognitive Assessment) — 10 min; detects MCI; preferred for more thorough screening",
      "MMSE — less sensitive for MCI than MoCA"
    ],
    "depression": "GDS-15 (Geriatric Depression Scale) — 15-item; specifically validated in older adults; scores ≥6 = probable depression"
  }
}

sections.heading: Management Approach
sections.body:
{
  "cga_components": [
    "1. Medication review: reconcile ALL medications including OTCs, supplements; apply Beers and STOPP criteria; deprescribe where possible",
    "2. Falls assessment: STEADI toolkit; TUG test; identify and address medications and environment",
    "3. Functional status: ADL/IADL screen; PT/OT referral if impairment",
    "4. Cognitive screen: Mini-Cog or MoCA; assess driving safety if cognitive impairment suspected",
    "5. Mood screen: GDS-15 or PHQ-9; depression is underdiagnosed and undertreated in older adults",
    "6. Nutritional status: MNA (Mini Nutritional Assessment); weight trend; albumin",
    "7. Social support: living situation, caregiver burden, community resources, advance directives"
  ],
  "prescribing_principles_older_adult": [
    "Start low, go slow — initial doses at 50% of standard adult dose for most medications",
    "Prioritize renal dosing: calculate CrCl (Cockcroft-Gault, not eGFR) for dosing — muscle mass is low in elderly, making creatinine-based eGFR unreliable",
    "Avoid Beers Criteria medications when alternatives exist",
    "Review anticholinergic burden at every visit (Anticholinergic Risk Scale or Anticholinergic Cognitive Burden Scale)",
    "Document the indication for every medication at initiation; reassess indication at every annual review"
  ]
}

sections.heading: Red Flags & Escalation
sections.body:
[
  "New fall with head injury → CT head to rule out subdural hematoma (even on anticoagulants)",
  "Acute functional decline (ADLs declining over days to 2 weeks) → rule out acute infection, medication toxicity, MI, acute delirium",
  "MoCA score <18 + driving → assess driving safety; consider formal evaluation; document discussion; notify DMV if required by state",
  "Severe frailty + acute illness → goals of care discussion; palliative care referral; avoid aggressive interventions without consent",
  "Multiple falls + anticoagulant + high bleeding risk → risk-benefit review of anticoagulation"
]

data.title: Deprescribing in Older Adults: Beers Criteria and High-Risk Medications
data.seoTitle: Deprescribing in Older Adults: Beers Criteria and High-Risk Medicatio…
data.seoDescription: Advanced NP clinical review: Deprescribing in Older Adults: Beers Criteria and High-Risk Medications. Advanced prescribing, management, and decision-making for…

sections.heading: Clinical Framing
sections.body: Medications cause more hospital admissions in older adults than most individual diseases. The American Geriatrics Society Beers Criteria is a validated, regularly updated list of medications that are potentially inappropriate in older adults (PIMs). NPs are uniquely positioned to deprescribe — often patients and other providers are reluctant to stop medications. Knowing WHAT to deprescribe, HOW to taper safely, and HOW to communicate the rationale to patients is essential clinical skill.

sections.heading: Core Concepts & Differential
Management Approach
Red Flags & Escalation
sections.body:
[
  "Benzodiazepine withdrawal: seizures, delirium tremens equivalent (if high-dose, long-duration use) → do NOT abruptly stop; escalate to addiction medicine or hospital if high-risk",
  "Antipsychotic withdrawal in dementia: behavioral rebound — monitor closely; very gradual taper",
  "Statin discontinuation in high ASCVD risk patient: discuss risk explicitly; document patient preference",
  "Warfarin discontinuation: verify AF/VTE indication is truly resolved; consult cardiology if uncertain"
]

data.title: Psoriasis and Atopic Dermatitis: Stepwise Treatment and Biologic Referral Criteria
data.seoTitle: Psoriasis and Atopic Dermatitis: Stepwise Treatment and Biologic Refe…
data.seoDescription: Advanced NP clinical review: Psoriasis and Atopic Dermatitis: Stepwise Treatment and Biologic Referral Criteria. Clinical reasoning and management for NP prima…

sections.heading: Clinical Framing
sections.body: Psoriasis and atopic dermatitis (AD) are the two most common chronic inflammatory skin conditions managed in primary care. NPs should be competent to initiate and escalate topical therapy and recognize when patients need dermatology referral for systemic or biologic therapy. Both conditions have significant quality-of-life impact often underestimated in clinical encounters.

sections.heading: Core Concepts & Differential
sections.body:
{
  "psoriasis": {
    "presentation": "Well-demarcated, salmon-pink plaques with thick, silvery scale; Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding when scale removed); Koebner phenomenon (lesions at trauma sites); common on extensor surfaces (elbows, knees), scalp, lumbosacral, nails (pitting, onycholysis)",
    "psoriatic_arthritis": "Affects ~30% of psoriasis patients; asymmetric oligoarthritis, dactylitis ('sausage digit'), enthesitis, spondylitis; refer to rheumatology; joint destruction if untreated",
    "comorbidities": "Metabolic syndrome, cardiovascular disease, depression, inflammatory bowel disease — screen at annual visits",
    "severity_assessment": {
      "PASI": "Psoriasis Area and Severity Index: scores erythema, induration, desquamation across BSA; >10 = moderate-severe",
      "BSA": "Body surface area; >10% = moderate-severe (palm = ~1%)",
      "DLQI": "Dermatology Life Quality Index; >10 = significant QoL impact — biologic criteria"
    },
    "stepwiseTreatment": {
      "mild_topical": [
        "High-potency topical corticosteroids (betamethasone dipropionate, clobetasol propionate) — first-line; limit to 2 weeks on face/flexures; 4-8 weeks on body; use lowest potency that controls disease",
        "Vitamin D analogues (calcipotriol/calcipotriene): steroid-sparing, no atrophy risk; excellent for maintenance after steroid induction; combine with betamethasone dipropionate (Taclonex) for initial control",
        "Tazarotene (retinoid): for thick plaques; irritating — use with emollient; avoid pregnancy",
        "Coal tar: less cosmetically acceptable but effective for scalp psoriasis"
      ],
      "moderate_severe_systemic": [
        "Methotrexate (MTX): weekly dosing (7.5-25 mg/week PO or IM); monitor CBC, LFTs monthly initially; contraindicated in pregnancy (Category X); add folic acid 1 mg daily to reduce side effects",
        "Cyclosporine: rapid onset; monitor BP and renal function monthly; 2-year limit; bridges to biologic",
        "Acitretin (oral retinoid): teratogenic for 3 years after stopping in women; effective for palmoplantar and nail psoriasis"
      ],
      "biologics_referralCriteria": [
        "PASI >10 or BSA >10% or DLQI >10 (i.e., moderate-severe affecting quality of life)",
        "Failure of adequate trials of ≥2 topical therapies",
        "Psoriatic arthritis with active joint disease",
        "NPs should refer to dermatology or rheumatology for biologic initiation — these are prescribable by NPs in some states but require specialist workup in most settings"
      ],
      "biologic_classes": [
        "TNF-alpha inhibitors: adalimumab (Humira), etanercept — older class; effective; screen for TB and hepatitis B before initiation",
        "IL-17 inhibitors: secukinumab (Cosentyx), ixekizumab — highly effective for plaque psoriasis and PsA",
        "IL-23 inhibitors: risankizumab (Skyrizi), guselkumab — long dosing intervals; very high efficacy"
      ]
    }
  },
  "atopicDermatitis": {
    "presentation": "Intensely pruritic, eczematous plaques; chronic-relapsing; FLEXURAL distribution (antecubital/popliteal fossae, neck, wrists) in older children/adults; cheeks and extensor surfaces in infants; lichenification from chronic scratching; associated with atopic triad (asthma, allergic rhinitis)",
    "itchScratchCycle": "Pruritus → scratching → skin barrier disruption → secondary colonization (S. aureus in >90%) → worsening inflammation → worsening pruritus",
    "diagnosis": "Clinical diagnosis; no specific lab test. Elevated IgE and eosinophilia supportive but non-specific.",
    "severity": "Mild: limited areas, infrequent itching; Moderate: widespread, frequent itch, sleep disruption; Severe: widespread, constant itch, significant QoL impact",
    "stepwiseTreatment": {
      "foundation_allStages": [
        "Emollients: cornerstone of all AD management; apply within 3 minutes of bathing (soak-and-seal); thick creams or ointments (CeraVe, Vanicream, petroleum jelly) preferred over lotions",
        "Trigger avoidance: identify and avoid soaps/detergents, specific foods (if confirmed), environmental allergens, wool",
        "Gentle skin care: lukewarm water, fragrance-free detergents, pat dry"
      ],
      "mild_moderate_topical": [
        "Topical corticosteroids: dose frequency and potency based on severity and location; hydrocortisone 1% for face/flexures; triamcinolone 0.1% for trunk/extremities; fluocinonide or clobetasol for thick or resistant plaques — use pulse dosing to maintain remission",
        "Topical calcineurin inhibitors (TCIs): tacrolimus 0.03-0.1% ointment, pimecrolimus 1% cream — steroid-sparing; safe for face and eyelids; first-line for sensitive areas; black box warning for theoretical malignancy risk (clinical significance uncertain)",
        "Crisaborole (Eucrisa): PDE-4 inhibitor; mild-moderate AD; twice daily; low side-effect profile"
      ],
      "moderate_severe_systemic": [
        "Dupilumab (Dupixent): IL-4/IL-13 inhibitor; first biologic approved for AD; 300 mg SC every 2 weeks; dramatically effective; ophthalmology follow-up (conjunctivitis side effect); approved ≥6 months old",
        "JAK inhibitors (upadacitinib — Rinvoq; abrocitinib — Cibinqo): oral; very effective; monitor CBC, lipids, liver; not first-line for most; risk of serious infections, malignancy",
        "Cyclosporine: off-label; rapid onset; bridge to biologic",
        "Phototherapy: narrowband UVB; effective for widespread moderate AD"
      ],
      "biologic_referralCriteria": [
        "Inadequate response to topical therapy over 3-6 months",
        "Moderate-severe disease with significant QoL impact (SCORAD >50 or POEM >16)",
        "Frequent flares requiring systemic steroids (>2 courses/year)",
        "Refer to dermatology or allergy/immunology for dupilumab initiation and monitoring"
      ]
    },
    "infection_management": "S. aureus colonization is nearly universal — decolonization bleach baths (0.5 cup per full bathtub, 2-3x/week) reduce flares; treat secondary impetiginization with mupirocin topically (localized) or dicloxacillin/cephalexin orally (widespread)"
  }
}

sections.heading: Management Approach
Red Flags & Escalation
sections.body:
[
  "Erythroderma: >90% BSA involvement → ED (thermoregulation failure, sepsis risk)",
  "Eczema herpeticum (Kaposi varicelliform eruption): punched-out, clustered vesicular lesions + fever in AD patient → suspect HSV superinfection → acyclovir or valacyclovir; may require hospitalization",
  "Generalized pustular psoriasis: acute eruption of sterile pustules + fever + systemic illness → dermatology emergency",
  "Psoriatic arthritis joint destruction: aggressive joint swelling + elevated markers → rheumatology urgently"
]

data.title: Smoking Cessation: Pharmacotherapy Options and Counseling Strategies
data.seoTitle: Smoking Cessation: Pharmacotherapy Options and Counseling Strategies …
data.seoDescription: Advanced NP clinical review: Smoking Cessation: Pharmacotherapy Options and Counseling Strategies. Clinical reasoning and management for NP primary care practi…

sections.heading: Clinical Framing
sections.body: Tobacco use is the leading preventable cause of death in the US, responsible for 480,000 deaths/year. Smoking cessation is the single most cost-effective intervention across virtually every chronic disease. NPs have both the opportunity and the clinical mandate to address tobacco at every encounter. Brief cessation counseling in primary care combined with pharmacotherapy triples quit rates compared to willpower alone.

sections.heading: Core Concepts & Differential
Management Approach
Red Flags & Escalation
sections.body:
[
  "Varenicline: new onset or worsening depression, suicidal ideation, aggressive behavior → stop medication; refer to mental health",
  "Bupropion: new seizure → stop immediately; ER evaluation",
  "NRT in active ACS: use with caution — stop if chest pain worsens; NRT is safer than continued smoking but requires monitoring"
]

data.title: Diagnosing and Managing Knee Pain in the Outpatient NP Setting
data.seoTitle: Diagnosing and Managing Knee Pain in the Outpatient NP Setting | NP C…
data.seoDescription: NP advanced review: Diagnosing and Managing Knee Pain in the Outpatient NP Setting. Clinical reasoning for NP primary care practice.

sections.heading: Learning Objectives
sections.body:
[
  "Distinguish structural, inflammatory, and referred causes of knee pain using history and targeted physical exam",
  "Apply the Ottawa Knee Rules to determine imaging necessity",
  "Identify the red flags that mandate joint aspiration or emergency referral",
  "Select appropriate first-line management for OA, gout, and patellofemoral pain"
]

sections.heading: Structural and Mechanical Causes
sections.body:
[
  "Osteoarthritis: age >45, insidious onset, morning stiffness <30 min, crepitus, bony enlargement, no systemic features; X-ray confirms joint space narrowing in atypical cases",
  "Patellofemoral pain syndrome (PFPS): anterior knee pain worsened by stairs, squatting, and prolonged sitting (movie sign); Clarke's test positive; no effusion; young active patients",
  "Meniscal injury: medial or lateral joint line tenderness, McMurray's test positive, effusion common; acute tears may lock; MRI required before surgical decision",
  "Ligament sprains: mechanism-driven (valgus stress MCL, pivot-deceleration ACL); grade I-II MCL conservative; ACL complete tear requires orthopedic referral",
  "Prepatellar bursitis: discrete anterior patellar swelling; occupation-related (kneeling); aspirate only if septic bursitis suspected"
]

sections.heading: Inflammatory and Crystal Arthropathies
sections.body:
[
  "Gout: acute monoarthritis peaking in 24h, erythema, extreme tenderness; negatively birefringent needle-shaped urate crystals under polarized light; serum uric acid may be normal during acute attack",
  "Pseudogout (CPPD): clinically mimics gout; positively birefringent rhomboid crystals; chondrocalcinosis on X-ray; common in elderly; same acute treatment as gout",
  "Septic arthritis (EMERGENCY): fever + acute hot swollen joint; any age; risk factors include immunosuppression, recent injection, skin breach; WBC >50,000 in aspirate; joint aspiration is mandatory before empiric antibiotics",
  "Rheumatoid arthritis: symmetric polyarthritis, MCP/PIP/wrist predominance, stiffness >1h; RF and anti-CCP positive; knee involvement in established disease; refer to rheumatology"
]

sections.heading: Referred Pain Pattern
sections.body:
[
  "Hip OA commonly refers pain to anterior knee or medial thigh; knee exam is normal; restricted hip internal rotation on exam is the key clue",
  "L3-L4 lumbar radiculopathy may produce anterior thigh and knee pain; positive femoral nerve stretch test; associated low back pain",
  "Clinical rule: always examine the hip in every knee complaint, especially when knee findings do not match the symptom severity"
]

sections.heading: Ottawa Knee Rules and Imaging
sections.body:
[
  "X-ray indicated if any one criterion present: age ≥55, isolated patellar tenderness, fibular head tenderness, inability to flex to 90 degrees, inability to bear weight (4 steps) at time of injury AND in clinic",
  "MRI indicated for suspected ligament or meniscal injury in a surgical candidate, or when X-ray is negative but significant functional loss persists",
  "OA is a clinical diagnosis in most cases; imaging confirms but does not replace clinical judgment"
]

sections.heading: Management and Follow-Up
sections.body:
[
  "OA first-line: exercise therapy, weight loss, acetaminophen PRN; topical NSAIDs (diclofenac gel) preferred over oral NSAIDs in older adults with renal risk; intra-articular corticosteroid injections for moderate flares",
  "Gout acute: colchicine 0.6mg BID, or NSAIDs, or prednisone if contraindicated; long-term ULT (allopurinol) if ≥2 flares/year or tophi; target uric acid <6 mg/dL",
  "Septic arthritis: do not treat empirically without aspiration confirmation; send to ED for joint washout and IV antibiotics",
  "Escalation triggers: failure of 6-8 weeks conservative therapy, suspected surgical lesion, septic arthritis, new mechanical locking"
]

sections.heading: Teaching Notes
sections.body: The NP's highest-stakes error in knee pain is attributing fever and monoarthritis to gout without excluding septic arthritis. These can coexist. Any acute hot joint with fever requires aspiration. For OA: resist escalating to NSAIDs before recommending PT and weight loss — these have the best long-term outcomes. For referred pain: always examine the hip; a completely normal knee exam with anterior thigh pain points away from the knee.

data.title: Evidence-Based UTI Management with Stewardship Principles
data.seoTitle: Evidence-Based UTI Management with Stewardship Principles | NP Clinic…
data.seoDescription: NP advanced review: Evidence-Based UTI Management with Stewardship Principles. Clinical reasoning for NP primary care practice.

sections.heading: Learning Objectives
sections.body:
[
  "Classify UTI as uncomplicated or complicated using clinical criteria",
  "Select first-line antibiotic for uncomplicated cystitis using stewardship principles",
  "Apply pregnancy-specific treatment and screening protocols",
  "Avoid overtreating asymptomatic bacteriuria in elderly and non-pregnant adults"
]

sections.heading: Uncomplicated vs Complicated UTI
sections.body:
[
  "Uncomplicated: dysuria, frequency, urgency in a non-pregnant healthy adult woman with no structural abnormalities; clinical diagnosis acceptable without culture",
  "Complicated: any of — male sex, pregnancy, diabetes, indwelling catheter, structural abnormality, immunocompromise, symptoms >7 days, prior treatment failure, recent urinary instrumentation; culture mandatory",
  "Pyelonephritis features: fever ≥38°C, costovertebral angle tenderness, nausea/vomiting, systemic illness; WBC casts on urinalysis are pathognomonic for upper tract involvement"
]

sections.heading: Antibiotic Selection and Stewardship
sections.body:
[
  "Uncomplicated cystitis first-line: nitrofurantoin monohydrate/macrocrystals 100mg BID x5 days — preferred for low resistance, minimal collateral damage; AVOID if CrCl <30 or pyelonephritis (poor tissue penetration)",
  "TMP-SMX DS BID x3 days — use only when local resistance <20%; avoid first trimester and at term",
  "Fosfomycin 3g sachet single dose — convenient; effective against E. coli; less effective for Klebsiella",
  "Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin): NEVER first-line for uncomplicated cystitis; reserve for complicated UTI and pyelonephritis; high collateral damage, tendinopathy risk",
  "Pyelonephritis oral outpatient: ciprofloxacin 500mg BID x7 days or TMP-SMX DS BID x14 days (culture-guided); admit if unable to tolerate orals, pregnant, or severely ill"
]

sections.heading: Pregnancy-Specific Considerations
sections.body:
[
  "Screen all pregnant patients for ASB at first prenatal visit; treat all positive cultures regardless of symptoms — reduces pyelonephritis and preterm labor",
  "Safe antibiotics in pregnancy: nitrofurantoin (2nd trimester preferred; avoid ≥36 weeks), cephalexin (all trimesters), amoxicillin-clavulanate",
  "Avoid in pregnancy: TMP-SMX (1st trimester folate antagonism; late term kernicterus risk), fluoroquinolones (theoretical cartilage risk)",
  "Pyelonephritis in pregnancy: hospitalize in most cases; risk of preterm labor and sepsis"
]

sections.heading: Asymptomatic Bacteriuria — When Not to Treat
sections.body:
[
  "ASB is extremely common in elderly (>50% of institutionalized women) and catheterized patients; treatment increases resistance without benefit",
  "Treat ASB ONLY in: pregnancy, and pre-urological procedure patients",
  "Bacteriuria plus non-specific symptoms (confusion, falls) in elderly does not equal UTI — evaluate for other causes before prescribing antibiotics",
  "Catheter-associated UTI (CAUTI): treat only if symptomatic; remove or replace catheter if possible; culture-directed 5-7 days"
]

sections.heading: Recurrent UTI Prevention
sections.body:
[
  "Definition: ≥2 UTIs in 6 months or ≥3 in 12 months; send urine culture each episode",
  "Postmenopausal women: topical vaginal estrogen cream is the most effective evidence-based non-antibiotic prevention strategy",
  "Post-coital prophylaxis: single dose nitrofurantoin or TMP-SMX after intercourse if coitally related",
  "Continuous low-dose prophylaxis: nitrofurantoin 50-100mg nightly x6-12 months for frequent recurrence"
]

sections.heading: Teaching Notes
sections.body: The two most important stewardship mistakes in UTI: (1) prescribing fluoroquinolones for uncomplicated cystitis — this is a top antibiotic stewardship violation; (2) treating asymptomatic bacteriuria in the elderly without symptoms. In pregnancy, the calculus reverses — ASB always requires treatment. For the NP, developing a reflexive pattern of 'uncomplicated → nitrofurantoin, pyelonephritis → ciprofloxacin + culture first' covers the majority of presentations.

data.title: Comorbidity-Driven Drug Selection and Prescribing Specifics Beyond Metformin
data.seoTitle: Comorbidity-Driven Drug Selection and Prescribing Specifics Beyond Me…
data.seoDescription: NP advanced review: Comorbidity-Driven Drug Selection and Prescribing Specifics Beyond Metformin. Prescribing, management, and decision-making for NP boards.

sections.heading: Learning Objectives
sections.body:
[
  "Identify when T2DM intensification is indicated based on A1c targets and clinical context",
  "Select second-line agents using comorbidity-driven logic (ASCVD, HF, CKD, weight)",
  "Apply prescribing specifics for GLP-1 receptor agonists and SGLT-2 inhibitors including dosing and contraindications",
  "Individualize A1c targets in older adults and frail patients to avoid hypoglycemia harm"
]

sections.heading: When to Intensify
sections.body:
[
  "Intensify when A1c not at individualized goal after 3 months of optimized metformin",
  "At diagnosis: dual therapy or insulin if A1c ≥10% or symptomatic hyperglycemia (polyuria, weight loss, polydipsia)",
  "A1c targets: <7.0% for most healthy adults; 7.5-8.0% for complex older adults; <8.5% for frail with limited life expectancy — avoid hypoglycemia as primary harm in frail elderly"
]

sections.heading: GLP-1 Receptor Agonists — Prescribing Detail
sections.body:
[
  "Mechanism: incretin mimetic — glucose-dependent insulin secretion, glucagon suppression, delayed gastric emptying, satiety; low intrinsic hypoglycemia risk",
  "Semaglutide SC (Ozempic): start 0.25mg weekly x4 weeks → 0.5mg → 1mg → 2mg at 4-week intervals; proven MACE reduction in established ASCVD (SUSTAIN-6)",
  "Semaglutide oral (Rybelsus): 3mg daily x1 month → 7mg → 14mg; must take on empty stomach with ≤120mL water, no food for 30 minutes after",
  "Contraindications: personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma, MEN2 syndrome; use with caution in prior pancreatitis",
  "Side effects: nausea/vomiting/diarrhea during titration; rare pancreatitis and gallbladder disease; nausea resolves in 4-8 weeks for most patients"
]

sections.heading: SGLT-2 Inhibitors — Prescribing Detail
sections.body:
[
  "Mechanism: block renal glucose reabsorption (glycosuria), reduce sodium reabsorption; BP and weight reduction are secondary benefits",
  "Empagliflozin 10-25mg daily; dapagliflozin 10mg daily — both approved for HFrEF regardless of T2DM status",
  "CKD protection: dapagliflozin and empagliflozin slow GFR decline; renal benefit persists to eGFR ≥20 for HF/CKD indication; glycemic efficacy diminishes <45",
  "Hold 3 days before major surgery — euglycemic DKA risk",
  "Euglycemic DKA: normal or mildly elevated glucose with significant ketonemia — do not dismiss because glucose is normal; stop SGLT-2 and send to ED",
  "Contraindications: eGFR <30 for glycemic indication; Type 1 DM; recurrent genital mycotic infections (relative)"
]

sections.heading: Comorbidity-Driven Selection Matrix
sections.body:
[
  "Established ASCVD: GLP-1 RA (semaglutide, liraglutide) or SGLT-2 inhibitor — both reduce MACE; GLP-1 RA generally preferred for weight loss benefit",
  "HFrEF: SGLT-2 inhibitor first (dapagliflozin or empagliflozin) — strongest HHF reduction evidence; AVOID saxagliptin (increased HHF, SAVOR-TIMI) and pioglitazone (fluid retention)",
  "CKD stage 3b-4: SGLT-2 inhibitor for nephroprotection; GLP-1 RA safe; linagliptin preferred DPP-4 (no renal dose adjustment); avoid glyburide",
  "Weight loss priority: GLP-1 RA > SGLT-2 > DPP-4; avoid sulfonylureas and insulin if possible"
]

sections.heading: Older Adult Prescribing Adaptations
sections.body:
[
  "Hypoglycemia prevention is the primary goal in frail elderly — sulfonylureas (especially glyburide) and insulin carry high risk",
  "Preferred agents in elderly: linagliptin (no renal dose adjustment, no hypoglycemia), GLP-1 RA (no hypoglycemia, weight neutral at lower risk)",
  "Glyburide is Beers Criteria — avoid in all older adults; glipizide immediate release is less dangerous if sulfonylurea required",
  "Renal dosing: hold metformin if eGFR <30; sitagliptin requires dose reduction at CrCl <50; calculate CrCl by Cockcroft-Gault in elderly (eGFR overestimates function)"
]

sections.heading: Teaching Notes
sections.body: The paradigm shift in T2DM intensification: drug selection is now driven by comorbidity (ASCVD, HF, CKD, weight) more than glycemic potency alone. A patient with confirmed ASCVD or HFrEF has a clear evidence-based second agent. The NP must know saxagliptin + HF = contraindicated and pioglitazone + HF = contraindicated. Euglycemic DKA on SGLT-2 is the most frequently missed adverse effect — the key is: normal glucose does NOT exclude DKA in this drug class.

data.title: GOLD 2024 Classification, Inhaler Escalation, and Exacerbation Management
data.seoTitle: GOLD 2024 Classification, Inhaler Escalation, and Exacerbation Manage…
data.seoDescription: NP advanced review: GOLD 2024 Classification, Inhaler Escalation, and Exacerbation Management. Prescribing, management, and decision-making for NP boards.

sections.heading: Learning Objectives
sections.body:
[
  "Apply GOLD 2024 ABE classification to determine appropriate initial maintenance therapy",
  "Select long-acting bronchodilator class based on symptom burden and exacerbation history",
  "Use blood eosinophil count to guide ICS addition decisions",
  "Manage moderate COPD exacerbations outpatient with appropriate antibiotic and steroid selection"
]

sections.heading: Diagnosis and Spirometric Staging
sections.body:
[
  "Diagnosis requires post-bronchodilator spirometry: FEV1/FVC <0.70 — do NOT diagnose on symptoms alone",
  "GOLD spirometric grades: Grade 1 FEV1 ≥80%, Grade 2 FEV1 50-79%, Grade 3 FEV1 30-49%, Grade 4 FEV1 <30% predicted",
  "Spirometry grades inform prognosis but do NOT drive inhaler selection decisions — ABE classification does"
]

sections.heading: GOLD 2024 ABE Classification and Initial Inhaler Selection
sections.body:
[
  "ABE uses symptom burden (CAT ≥10 or mMRC ≥2 = more symptoms) AND exacerbation history (Group E = ≥2 moderate exacerbations OR ≥1 hospitalization)",
  "Group A: fewer symptoms, 0-1 uncomplicated exacerbations — any bronchodilator (LAMA preferred for persistent symptoms)",
  "Group B: more symptoms (CAT ≥10), 0-1 uncomplicated exacerbations — LAMA monotherapy; LAMA+LABA if highly symptomatic",
  "Group E: ≥2 moderate exacerbations or ≥1 hospitalization — LAMA+LABA dual bronchodilator as initial therapy; add ICS if eosinophils ≥300 or ongoing exacerbations",
  "LABA monotherapy is NOT preferred for COPD maintenance — LAMA has superior evidence; LABA alone is not recommended as single long-acting bronchodilator"
]

sections.heading: ICS Use — Eosinophil-Guided Decision
sections.body:
[
  "ICS is NOT first-line for all COPD — add only for eosinophilic phenotype or frequent exacerbators",
  "Add ICS when: blood eosinophils ≥300 cells/μL AND/OR Group E classification on dual bronchodilator",
  "ICS increases pneumonia risk — avoid in patients with recurrent pneumonia, eosinophils <100, or mycobacterial infection",
  "Triple therapy (LAMA+LABA+ICS): IMPACT trial showed mortality benefit in eosinophilic frequent exacerbators"
]

sections.heading: Exacerbation Management
sections.body:
[
  "Antibiotics indicated when Anthonisen criteria met: increased sputum volume + increased sputum purulence + increased dyspnea (all 3, or 2 of 3 in moderate-severe exacerbation)",
  "First-line antibiotic: azithromycin 500mg day 1 then 250mg x4 days; doxycycline 100mg BID x5 days; amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125mg BID x5-7 days",
  "Systemic steroids: prednisone 40mg daily x5 days — no benefit to longer courses (REDUCE trial); do not use inhaled ICS alone for exacerbations",
  "Oxygen target in known/suspected CO2 retainers: SpO2 88-92% — avoid driving above 92-94% to prevent CO2 narcosis from suppressed hypoxic drive",
  "Admission criteria: SpO2 <88% not responding to oxygen, acute respiratory failure, failure of outpatient therapy at 72h, inability to tolerate oral medications, poor social support"
]

sections.heading: Device Selection and Older Adult Considerations
sections.body:
[
  "Dry powder inhalers (DPIs) require sufficient inspiratory flow — in severe COPD or frail elderly, inadequate inspiratory effort limits delivery",
  "Soft-mist inhalers (SMIs, e.g., Spiriva Respimat) do not require high inspiratory flow — preferred when DPI technique is inadequate",
  "MDI with spacer improves delivery when coordination is poor; assess technique at every clinic visit",
  "Cardioselective beta-blockers are generally safe in COPD — do not withhold for CAD or HF management without compelling reason"
]

sections.heading: Teaching Notes
sections.body: Three common prescribing errors in COPD: (1) starting with SABA alone as sole long-term therapy — this is symptom-reactive, not maintenance; (2) adding ICS without eosinophilic indication — increases pneumonia risk; (3) continuing oral steroids beyond 5 days for exacerbation — no added benefit and significant harm. Oxygen targeting 88-92% is a critical outpatient skill for COPD patients who present in exacerbation.

data.title: Red Flag Identification and Safe Disposition in Headache Presentations
data.seoTitle: Red Flag Identification and Safe Disposition in Headache Presentation…
data.seoDescription: NP advanced review: Red Flag Identification and Safe Disposition in Headache Presentations. Prescribing, management, and decision-making for NP boards.

sections.heading: Learning Objectives
sections.body:
[
  "Apply the SNOOP4 framework to identify headache red flags requiring urgent evaluation",
  "Recognize thunderclap headache as SAH until proven otherwise and initiate the correct workup sequence",
  "Diagnose giant cell arteritis and initiate same-day treatment to prevent vision loss",
  "Differentiate migraine, tension-type, and cluster headache and select appropriate management"
]

sections.heading: SNOOP4 Red Flag Framework
sections.body:
[
  "S — Systemic symptoms: fever, stiff neck, rash, weight loss, immunocompromise",
  "N — Neurological signs: focal deficit, diplopia, papilledema, altered mentation",
  "O — Onset: thunderclap (maximal in seconds) — SAH until proven otherwise",
  "O2 — Older age: new headache pattern after age 50 (mass, GCA, malignancy)",
  "P — Papilledema: elevated ICP",
  "P2 — Positional: worse supine (ICP elevation) or upright (low CSF pressure)",
  "P3 — Progressive worsening over days to weeks",
  "P4 — Pattern change: significant change from patient's established headache"
]

sections.heading: Secondary Headache Emergencies
sections.body:
[
  "SAH: thunderclap onset, maximal intensity in seconds to 1 minute; workup: non-contrast CT head first (90-98% sensitive <6h), then LP for xanthochromia if CT negative; CT alone does NOT exclude SAH — LP is required with high clinical suspicion",
  "GCA (Temporal arteritis): age >50, new temporal headache, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, ESR >50, elevated CRP; action: start prednisone 40-60mg/day IMMEDIATELY — do not wait for biopsy; biopsy remains valid for 2 weeks after steroid initiation; vision loss from AION is irreversible",
  "Bacterial meningitis: fever + headache + neck stiffness + photophobia; petechial rash = meningococcemia requiring immediate antibiotics; CT first only if papilledema or focal deficit present; do not delay antibiotics for imaging in immunocompetent patient with classic triad",
  "PComm aneurysm: fixed dilated non-reactive ipsilateral pupil (mydriasis, not miosis) + headache = CN III compression; CT angiography urgently; do NOT treat as migraine",
  "CO poisoning: headache in enclosed space, multiple people affected, improves with fresh air; standard SpO2 useless (CO-oximetry required)"
]

sections.heading: Primary Headache Classification
sections.body:
[
  "Migraine: POUND mnemonic — Pulsating, 4-72 hOurs, Unilateral, Nausea, Disabling; photophobia/phonophobia; aura in 30%; classic presentation in reproductive-age women",
  "Tension-type: bilateral band-like, mild-moderate, no nausea, not worsened by activity; most common headache type overall; acetaminophen or NSAIDs first-line",
  "Cluster: periorbital unilateral, 15-180 min, 1-8/day, autonomic features (lacrimation, rhinorrhea, ptosis, Horner's), restlessness (pathognomonic); predominantly male; high-flow O2 + injectable sumatriptan are first-line acute treatments",
  "Medication overuse headache (MOH): analgesic/triptan use ≥10-15 days/month for >3 months; daily or near-daily headache pattern; treatment is analgesic withdrawal + preventive therapy"
]

sections.heading: Migraine Management
sections.body:
[
  "Acute mild-moderate: NSAIDs or acetaminophen; acute moderate-severe: triptans (sumatriptan 50-100mg PO or 6mg SC) — fastest onset SC; gepants (rimegepant) safe in cardiovascular disease",
  "Prophylaxis indications: ≥4 migraines/month, migraines lasting >48h, or significant disability (MIDAS ≥11)",
  "Prophylaxis options: topiramate 25-100mg/day, propranolol 40-240mg/day, amitriptyline 25-75mg nightly, CGRP monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, fremanezumab)",
  "Avoid in women of reproductive age: valproate (Category X teratogen) and topiramate (also teratogenic — REMS program, contraceptive counseling required)"
]

sections.heading: Teaching Notes
sections.body: The NP's primary obligation in headache is not to diagnose the headache type but to reliably identify who cannot be safely managed outpatient. First thunderclap headache = SAH protocol regardless of what the patient calls it. GCA requires same-day steroids before biopsy — vision loss is the permanent consequence of delay. Cluster headache is frequently misdiagnosed as migraine or trigeminal neuralgia; the restlessness (cannot sit still) differentiates it from migraine (patients want to lie in a dark quiet room).

data.title: Risk Stratification and Disposition in Syncope
data.seoTitle: Risk Stratification and Disposition in Syncope | NP Clinical Review
data.seoDescription: NP advanced review: Risk Stratification and Disposition in Syncope. Prescribing, management, and decision-making for NP boards.

sections.heading: Learning Objectives
sections.body:
[
  "Identify clinical features that distinguish vasovagal from cardiac syncope",
  "Apply validated risk stratification tools to determine safe outpatient vs ED disposition",
  "Recognize ECG patterns that mandate emergency referral",
  "Manage vasovagal syncope conservatively with education and counterpressure techniques"
]

sections.heading: Vasovagal vs Cardiac Syncope — Discriminating Features
sections.body:
[
  "Vasovagal: prodrome required (nausea, diaphoresis, blurred vision lasting seconds to minutes); positional trigger (prolonged standing, hot environment); situational (pain, emotion, blood draw); brief LOC <60 seconds; rapid complete recovery",
  "Cardiac arrhythmic: no prodrome or brief (<5 sec); occurs seated or supine; palpitations preceding LOC; prolonged recovery; exertional syncope; family history of sudden cardiac death",
  "Postictal confusion >5-10 minutes after regaining consciousness is the most discriminating feature for seizure over syncope; brief myoclonic jerks during syncope (anoxic myoclonus, <10 sec) do NOT indicate epilepsy",
  "Orthostatic hypotension: defined as BP drop ≥20 mmHg systolic or ≥10 mmHg diastolic within 3 minutes of standing; common causes — dehydration, medications (antihypertensives, alpha-blockers, diuretics, TCAs), autonomic neuropathy"
]

sections.heading: ECG Red Flags Requiring ED Referral
sections.body:
[
  "QTc prolongation >470ms (female) or >450ms (male) — risk of torsades de pointes and polymorphic VT",
  "Brugada pattern: coved ST elevation in V1-V2 with RBBB morphology",
  "Delta wave (short PR + slurred QRS upstroke) — WPW; high risk if AF develops",
  "Epsilon wave in V1-V3 — ARVC (arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy)",
  "Complete heart block or pauses >3 seconds on monitoring",
  "New LBBB in the context of syncope — consider structural heart disease"
]

sections.heading: Structural Cardiac Syncope — High-Risk Pattern
sections.body:
[
  "Exertional syncope = structural cardiac disease until proven otherwise: HOCM, severe aortic stenosis, pulmonary arterial hypertension",
  "HOCM is the most common cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes in the US; outflow obstruction worsens with exertion",
  "Mandatory workup for exertional syncope: ECG, echocardiogram, same-day or next-day cardiology; no return to sports without clearance",
  "Family history of sudden cardiac death + young patient + exertional syncope = emergent evaluation"
]

sections.heading: Risk Stratification and Disposition
sections.body:
[
  "High-risk features mandating ED or hospital: exertional syncope, abnormal ECG, structural heart disease, no prodrome in older adult, troponin elevation, HF signs, oxygen saturation <94%",
  "Low-risk (outpatient safe): age <40, classic vasovagal story, normal ECG, no structural disease, normal vital signs, symmetric prodrome",
  "San Francisco Syncope Rule high-risk: HF history, hematocrit <30%, abnormal ECG, shortness of breath, SBP <90 at presentation",
  "ECG is mandatory for ALL patients presenting with syncope — this is the single most important initial test"
]

sections.heading: Vasovagal Management
sections.body:
[
  "Education and reassurance: benign prognosis when thoroughly evaluated and no cardiac etiology",
  "Counterpressure maneuvers (leg crossing, arm tensing, squatting when prodrome begins) — proven to abort or delay many episodes",
  "Increase fluid and salt intake if not contraindicated; avoid prolonged standing, hot environments, dehydration",
  "Midodrine 5-10mg TID (alpha-agonist) for recurrent orthostatic-vasovagal; fludrocortisone 0.1mg daily for volume-depleted phenotype",
  "Driving restriction: document discussion; most states require reporting to DMV for recurrent or high-risk syncope"
]

sections.heading: Teaching Notes
sections.body: The urgent care NP's primary role in syncope is not to diagnose the exact mechanism but to triage: who goes to the ED now, who sees cardiology tomorrow, and who can be safely reassured. A normal ECG and classic vasovagal story in a young person is genuinely low-risk. The same presentation in an older adult or anyone with structural disease is not. The mandatory ECG is non-negotiable.

data.title: Distinguishing Hypertensive Emergency from Urgency and Directing Appropriate Care
data.seoTitle: Distinguishing Hypertensive Emergency from Urgency and Directing Appr…
data.seoDescription: NP advanced review: Distinguishing Hypertensive Emergency from Urgency and Directing Appropriate Care. Clinical reasoning for NP primary care practice.

sections.heading: Learning Objectives
sections.body:
[
  "Define hypertensive emergency versus urgency using end-organ damage criteria",
  "Identify the most dangerous individual presentations within hypertensive emergency",
  "Avoid common errors including sublingual nifedipine use and premature aggressive lowering in urgency",
  "Recognize resistant hypertension and initiate secondary workup"
]

sections.heading: Emergency vs Urgency — The Defining Distinction
sections.body:
[
  "Hypertensive urgency: BP ≥180/120 WITHOUT acute end-organ damage; patient may have headache or anxiety; managed with oral agents, rest, and 24-48h follow-up",
  "Hypertensive emergency: BP ≥180/120 WITH acute end-organ damage — brain, eye, heart, kidney, aorta, placenta; requires ED transfer for IV management",
  "End-organ damage markers: new confusion/altered mentation, papilledema, flash pulmonary edema, ACS (chest pain + ECG changes), aortic dissection (tearing pain + inter-arm differential), AKI (creatinine doubling), eclampsia"
]

sections.heading: Specific Hypertensive Emergency Presentations
sections.body:
[
  "Aortic dissection: tearing ripping chest or back pain + inter-arm systolic BP difference >20 mmHg; call 911; target SBP <120 with IV beta-blocker FIRST (esmolol or labetalol) to reduce HR AND BP, then vasodilator — NEVER vasodilator alone (reflex tachycardia increases aortic wall shear force)",
  "Hypertensive encephalopathy: confusion + severe headache + elevated BP; PRES on MRI; reduce MAP 10-15% in first hour, then 15% over next 23 hours — NOT rapid normalization",
  "Eclampsia: BP ≥160/110 + seizure or severe features in pregnant/postpartum patient; magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis; labetalol or hydralazine IV; call obstetrics and 911; delivery is definitive treatment",
  "HELLP syndrome: elevated BP + elevated LFTs + hemolysis + thrombocytopenia + RUQ pain in pregnancy/postpartum — obstetric emergency"
]

sections.heading: Urgency Management — What NOT to Do
sections.body:
[
  "Do NOT administer sublingual nifedipine — this causes rapid uncontrolled BP drop precipitating stroke and MI; it is dangerous and specifically contraindicated",
  "Do NOT rapidly lower BP in urgency — rapid lowering in chronically hypertensive patients causes ischemia of brain and heart (autoregulatory range reset)",
  "Correct approach: have patient rest 20-30 minutes in quiet room, restart or adjust oral antihypertensives, arrange 24-48h follow-up",
  "Urgency does NOT require hospital admission — close outpatient follow-up is appropriate when no end-organ damage is present"
]

sections.heading: Resistant Hypertension
sections.body:
[
  "Definition: BP above goal despite ≥3 antihypertensive agents at maximum tolerated doses including a diuretic, with confirmed adherence",
