Roflumilast (A) is appropriate adjunct for patients with chronic bronchitis phenotype (productive cough) on maximal therapy — a reasonable consideration but not the most impactful single intervention compared to smoking cessation + rehab. Azithromycin prophylaxis (B) is evidence-based for chronic macrolide use in frequent exacerbators — also reasonable but carries QTc and hearing risks; requires careful patient selection; secondary option. Higher ICS dose (D) has no evidence for reducing exacerbations beyond standard doses.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Roflumilast is a valid add-on for chronic bronchitis phenotype but is secondary to addressing smoking and rehabilitation.
incorrectAnswerRationale.B: Chronic azithromycin is a valid option but requires QTc and hearing monitoring and is not the most impactful step without addressing smoking.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Higher ICS dose without eosinophil-guided evidence does not reduce exacerbations and increases pneumonia risk.

data.stem: A 44-year-old woman presents to your urgent care clinic reporting 'the worst headache of my life.' It began suddenly while she was exercising, reached maximum intensity within 60 seconds, and is now 9/10. She has no prior headache history. Neurological exam is normal. Temperature 37.0°C. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate IMMEDIATE next step?
options: Diagnose migraine — new onset migraine often presents severely and is exacerbated by physical activity
Order sumatriptan 6 mg SC to abort what is likely a migraine attack
Transfer to ED immediately for non-contrast CT head; if CT negative, proceed to lumbar puncture
Order MRI of the brain — more sensitive than CT for subarachnoid hemorrhage
correctAnswer: Transfer to ED immediately for non-contrast CT head; if CT negative, proceed to lumbar puncture
data.rationale: Thunderclap headache ('worst headache of my life,' reaching maximum intensity within seconds to minutes) is a SAH until proven otherwise. SAH is a neurosurgical emergency with 30-day mortality of 30-40%; those who survive often have severe deficits. The diagnostic algorithm: non-contrast CT head FIRST (90-98% sensitive within 6 hours of SAH onset); if CT is negative but clinical suspicion remains high (which it does here — classic thunderclap) → lumbar puncture to detect xanthochromia or persistent elevated RBCs that indicate SAH missed by CT. This patient MUST be transferred to the ED — she cannot be managed in urgent care. MRI (D) is less sensitive than CT for acute SAH (poor detection of subarachnoid blood in first 24 hours) and takes longer — CT is the correct first test. Migraine diagnosis (A and B) must never be made in the setting of a first thunderclap headache without exclusion of SAH.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: First thunderclap headache cannot be diagnosed as migraine without ruling out SAH — this is a potentially fatal error.
incorrectAnswerRationale.B: Administering a triptan without excluding SAH is inappropriate and dangerous in thunderclap headache.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: MRI is less sensitive than CT for acute subarachnoid hemorrhage and takes longer — CT is the preferred first imaging modality.

data.stem: A 67-year-old woman presents with a new headache over the past 3 weeks, predominantly over the right temple. She reports jaw fatigue when chewing and scalp tenderness when combing her hair. ESR is 88 mm/hr, CRP is markedly elevated. What is the MOST appropriate immediate management?
options: Order temporal artery biopsy today; await results before starting steroids to avoid invalidating the biopsy
Start high-dose prednisone 40-60 mg daily TODAY; schedule temporal artery biopsy within 2 weeks
Refer to ophthalmology for urgent fundoscopy; start steroids only if papilledema is found
Order CTA of the head and neck; start aspirin 81 mg daily for presumed arteritis
correctAnswer: Start high-dose prednisone 40-60 mg daily TODAY; schedule temporal artery biopsy within 2 weeks
data.rationale: This is giant cell arteritis (GCA, temporal arteritis) until proven otherwise — a vascular inflammatory emergency where the primary threat is irreversible vision loss (anterior ischemic optic neuropathy). GCA presents in patients >50 with temporal headache, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, elevated ESR (>50 mm/hr), and elevated CRP. The cardinal rule: START HIGH-DOSE PREDNISONE IMMEDIATELY — do not wait for biopsy results. Temporal artery biopsy remains the gold standard diagnostic test but should be done within 2 weeks of starting steroids (histologic findings remain positive for up to 2-4 weeks on steroids). Delaying steroids to avoid 'invalidating the biopsy' (A) risks permanent blindness — the biopsy is NOT invalidated within 2 weeks of steroid initiation. Ophthalmology referral (C) is appropriate but not before starting steroids. CTA and aspirin (D) are adjuncts — treatment is systemic corticosteroids, not aspirin.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Never delay steroids waiting for biopsy in suspected GCA — vision loss can occur within hours to days.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: Starting steroids should not be conditional on finding papilledema — GCA is a clinical diagnosis supported by labs.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: CTA can help diagnose large-vessel GCA but starting aspirin alone is not the treatment — high-dose steroids are required.

data.stem: A 32-year-old woman with a 5-year history of migraines presents with a headache she describes as 'just my usual migraine' but more severe than typical. She has had no fever or stiff neck. Exam shows a right-sided pupil that is 6 mm and non-reactive to light. The left pupil is 3 mm and reactive. Which is the MOST appropriate next step?
options: Treat as migraine with triptan — pupil asymmetry can occur in migraine with aura
Transfer to ED immediately for urgent CT angiography — asymmetric pupil suggests posterior communicating artery aneurysm
Check for prior Horner's syndrome in the chart and reassure if documented history
Refer to ophthalmology for next-week appointment for formal pupil assessment
correctAnswer: Transfer to ED immediately for urgent CT angiography — asymmetric pupil suggests posterior communicating artery aneurysm
data.rationale: An isolated fixed dilated pupil (mydriasis) with a contralateral normal pupil and headache = posterior communicating artery (PComm) aneurysm compressing the oculomotor nerve (CN III) until proven otherwise. CN III compression by a PComm aneurysm preferentially affects the parasympathetic pupillomotor fibers (on the outer surface), causing ipsilateral mydriasis and ptosis. This is a sentinel warning sign of impending aneurysm rupture → subarachnoid hemorrhage. Even in a patient with known migraines, a new neurological sign (anisocoria with unreactive dilated pupil) is a SNOOP4 red flag mandating immediate CT angiography to rule out aneurysm. Treating as migraine (A) risks missing an about-to-rupture aneurysm. Horner's syndrome (C) causes a SMALL pupil (miosis) with partial ptosis, not a large dilated non-reactive pupil. Ophthalmology referral next week (D) is dangerously delayed.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Migraine with aura can cause visual changes but does NOT cause a fixed dilated pupil — this is a neurological emergency.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: Horner's syndrome causes miosis (small pupil), not mydriasis (large pupil) — opposite finding.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: A fixed dilated pupil in the context of headache is an emergency — next-week referral is unacceptably delayed.

data.stem: A 28-year-old woman on oral contraceptive pills has an established diagnosis of migraine with aura (visual scotoma preceding each headache). She was started on sumatriptan for acute attacks 1 year ago. At today's visit she asks about preventive therapy — she has 6 migraines per month, each lasting 24-36 hours. Which preventive agent is CONTRAINDICATED in this specific patient?
options: Topiramate
Valproic acid
Propranolol
Erenumab (CGRP monoclonal antibody)
correctAnswer: Valproic acid
data.rationale: Valproic acid (valproate) is a teratogen (Category X): associated with neural tube defects, cognitive impairment, and neonatal hepatotoxicity. It is CONTRAINDICATED in women of reproductive age unless highly effective contraception is in place AND other options have failed, with thorough informed consent. While this patient is on OCP, the FDA REMS program (SHARE) for valproate requires specific documentation. In a 28-year-old with established migraine, valproate should be avoided when alternatives exist. Topiramate (A) is also teratogenic but is classified as 'use with caution and contraception' rather than absolute contraindication in managed settings — still a concern, but the question asks which is CONTRAINDICATED specifically. Propranolol (C) is safe in most women not trying to conceive; avoid in pregnancy but not in women on OCP. Erenumab (D) is a CGRP monoclonal antibody approved for migraine prevention — relatively newer, Category X in pregnancy by convention but commonly used in reproductive-age women with appropriate counseling.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Topiramate is teratogenic but the AAN guidelines specifically list valproate as the agent to avoid in women of childbearing potential without REMS.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: Propranolol has a reasonable safety profile for preventive use in women on OCP.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Erenumab is an acceptable preventive option in reproductive-age women with appropriate counseling about stopping before pregnancy.

data.stem: A 36-year-old man presents with headaches occurring in clusters every spring for the past 4 years. Each attack is described as periorbital, unilateral, excruciating, lasting 45 minutes, occurring 2-3 times per day. During attacks he paces the room and is unable to sit still. He has ipsilateral lacrimation, nasal congestion, and ptosis during attacks. Which acute treatment is MOST effective for this patient?
options: Oral sumatriptan 100 mg — first-line triptan for all headache types
High-flow 100% oxygen 7-15 L/min via non-rebreather mask for 15 minutes; injectable sumatriptan 6 mg SC as alternative
Prochlorperazine 10 mg IV — effective for all primary headache acute treatment
Butalbital/acetaminophen/caffeine — appropriate for severe primary headaches
correctAnswer: High-flow 100% oxygen 7-15 L/min via non-rebreather mask for 15 minutes; injectable sumatriptan 6 mg SC as alternative
data.rationale: This is cluster headache: periodic pattern (spring clustering), periorbital unilateral severe pain, autonomic features (lacrimation, rhinorrhea, ptosis, Horner's), short duration (15-180 min), restlessness (pathognomonic — patients cannot stay still, unlike migraine). First-line acute treatments are (1) high-flow 100% oxygen 7-15 L/min via non-rebreather for 15 minutes (fastest and safest), and (2) injectable sumatriptan 6 mg SC (fastest medication onset). Oral sumatriptan (A) has too slow an onset (30-60 min) for cluster attacks lasting only 15-180 minutes — absorption too slow relative to attack duration. Prochlorperazine (C) is used IV for migraine in ED settings, not outpatient cluster management. Butalbital compounds (D) are never appropriate for cluster headaches — no evidence, high abuse potential.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Oral sumatriptan is too slow for cluster attacks — injectable SC or nasal spray are preferred.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: Prochlorperazine is useful for migraine-associated nausea, not cluster headache.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Butalbital compounds have no role in cluster headache treatment.

data.stem: A 42-year-old woman reports headaches present nearly every day for the past 4 months. She was previously episodic (2-3 headaches/month). She uses ibuprofen 400 mg or sumatriptan 50 mg on 14-18 days per month. She describes the daily headaches as dull but has superimposed severe attacks 3-4 times per month. Which diagnosis BEST explains her current headache pattern?
options: Migraine with aura that has evolved to chronic migraine
Medication overuse headache (MOH) — the frequent analgesic/triptan use is driving the daily pattern
New daily persistent headache — requires MRI to rule out secondary cause
Cluster headache variant — increased frequency suggests a new cluster period
correctAnswer: Medication overuse headache (MOH) — the frequent analgesic/triptan use is driving the daily pattern
data.rationale: Medication overuse headache (MOH, previously 'analgesic rebound') develops when patients use acute headache medications ≥10 days/month (triptans, ergotamines) or ≥15 days/month (NSAIDs, acetaminophen) for >3 months. The result is a paradoxical daily or near-daily headache driven by medication overuse. This patient uses treatment agents on 14-18 days/month for 4 months — classic MOH. Treatment requires withdrawal of the offending analgesic (can taper NSAIDs; triptans can be stopped abruptly). The initial withdrawal phase worsens headache for 7-10 days. Concurrently, initiate prophylactic therapy. Chronic migraine (A) is a valid concern but MOH is the primary driver that must be addressed first — removing the MOH often reveals the true underlying pattern. MRI (C) is indicated for new daily headache with red flags but not as initial step in this clearly explained MOH pattern. Cluster (D) has distinctly different features (periorbital, autonomic, restlessness).

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Chronic migraine can coexist but MOH from medication overuse must be addressed first — MOH explains the daily pattern.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: MRI is not the priority when MOH is the most likely diagnosis; investigate further if pattern doesn't improve after medication withdrawal.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Cluster headache is episodic with severe unilateral periorbital pain and autonomic features — does not match this daily dull pattern.

data.stem: A 55-year-old immunocompromised (HIV, CD4 120) man presents with 5 days of progressive headache, low-grade fever (37.8°C), and subtle confusion. His wife says he has been 'not himself.' Neck stiffness is present on exam. Which is the MOST appropriate next step?
options: Order CT head first; if no mass effect or herniation signs → perform lumbar puncture; start empiric antibiotics + antifungal simultaneously
Administer oral ciprofloxacin empirically for presumed bacterial meningitis and arrange outpatient neurology
Start empiric IV ceftriaxone before any imaging — do not delay for CT
Discharge with analgesics and close follow-up — HIV patients frequently have headaches
correctAnswer: Order CT head first; if no mass effect or herniation signs → perform lumbar puncture; start empiric antibiotics + antifungal simultaneously
data.rationale: In an immunocompromised patient with headache + fever + altered mental status + neck stiffness, the differential includes bacterial meningitis AND cryptococcal meningitis (Cryptococcus neoformans is the most common cause of meningitis in HIV patients with CD4 <200). Management: CT head first to rule out mass lesion or herniation before LP (immunocompromised patients have higher risk of CNS mass lesions). If CT clear → LP with India ink preparation (50% sensitivity for cryptococcal meningitis), cryptococcal antigen (>90% sensitivity), standard bacterial culture, and opening pressure. Start empiric antibiotics AND antifungal (amphotericin B + flucytosine for cryptococcal meningitis; ceftriaxone + dexamethasone for bacterial). Oral ciprofloxacin (B) is wholly inadequate for meningitis. Starting ceftriaxone before CT (C) applies to immunocompetent patients with no papilledema — in immunocompromised, CT first is the guideline to rule out mass lesion. Discharge (D) is inappropriate with meningeal signs.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Correct — this is the appropriate sequence and empiric coverage for immunocompromised meningitis.
incorrectAnswerRationale.B: Oral antibiotics are not appropriate for bacterial or cryptococcal meningitis.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: Immunocompromised patients should have CT before LP due to higher risk of CNS mass lesions causing herniation.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Meningeal signs and altered mental status in an immunocompromised patient is a medical emergency.

data.stem: An 18-year-old male basketball player collapsed during a game. Witnesses report he was running and suddenly fell without any warning prodrome. He regained consciousness within 1 minute and feels fine now. He has no prior syncopal episodes. Vital signs are normal. He has a family history of his uncle dying of sudden cardiac death at age 35. ECG shows voltage criteria for left ventricular hypertrophy. Which is the MOST appropriate management?
options: Reassure — vasovagal syncope is most common in young athletes; clear him to return to sport
Transfer to ED or same-day cardiology referral; restrict all vigorous physical activity until workup complete
Schedule ECG and echocardiogram in 4-6 weeks — exertional syncope is common in athletes
Prescribe midodrine for orthostatic hypotension — likely dehydration during exercise
correctAnswer: Transfer to ED or same-day cardiology referral; restrict all vigorous physical activity until workup complete
data.rationale: This young athlete has EXERTIONAL SYNCOPE — the highest-risk syncope scenario. Combined with: no prodrome, family history of sudden cardiac death at 35, and ECG showing LVH — HOCM (hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy) must be excluded urgently. HOCM is the leading cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes in the US. Exertional syncope with LVH on ECG is an absolute contraindication to sports participation until structural cardiac disease is excluded. Same-day cardiology referral + echocardiogram is mandatory. Clearing to return to sport (A) without workup is dangerous and potentially catastrophic. A 4-6 week wait (C) is unacceptably delayed for exertional syncope with a high-risk presentation. Midodrine (D) treats orthostatic hypotension — orthostasis does not cause syncope during exertion without prodrome.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Exertional syncope without prodrome + family history of sudden cardiac death + LVH on ECG = high-risk; cannot be managed as vasovagal.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: 4-6 week delay in evaluating potential HOCM with activity restrictions not enforced is unacceptably dangerous.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Orthostatic hypotension does not cause exertional syncope without prodrome while running.

data.stem: Which of the following ECG findings in a patient presenting with syncope is an ABSOLUTE indication for ED referral?
options: Sinus bradycardia at 56 bpm with normal PR and QRS
QTc of 510 ms
Left axis deviation
T-wave inversions in V1-V2 only
correctAnswer: QTc of 510 ms
data.rationale: QTc prolongation of 510 ms is a high-risk finding that predisposes to torsades de pointes (polymorphic VT) and sudden cardiac death. A QTc >500 ms is associated with substantially increased risk of lethal arrhythmia and requires immediate ED referral. Normal QTc is ≤450 ms males, ≤470 ms females. Sinus bradycardia at 56 bpm (A) is a normal finding in athletes and does not independently mandate ED referral. Left axis deviation (C) is a common, nonspecific finding not independently high-risk for syncope. T-wave inversions in V1-V2 alone (D) can be normal (especially in women) or represent right heart strain — requires context but not automatically an ED indicator without other findings.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Mild bradycardia in athletes is physiologic — not independently high-risk in syncope.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: Left axis deviation is a common, nonspecific finding and not an indicator for ED in syncope.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Isolated T-wave inversions in V1-V2 alone are not high-risk; broader pattern or context would be needed.

data.stem: A 24-year-old woman passed out in church while standing for a long service on a hot day. Bystanders report she was pale, sweaty, and went limp for about 30 seconds. She woke up quickly and felt normal within a minute. She has no cardiac history, no medications, and her ECG is normal. What is the MOST appropriate management?
options: Admit to telemetry for 24-hour cardiac monitoring — all first-time syncope requires observation
Refer to cardiology urgently — first syncope episode always requires specialist evaluation
Diagnose vasovagal syncope clinically; educate on counterpressure maneuvers, hydration, and avoidance of prolonged standing in hot environments; close outpatient follow-up
Order Holter monitor immediately and prescribe midodrine empirically
correctAnswer: Diagnose vasovagal syncope clinically; educate on counterpressure maneuvers, hydration, and avoidance of prolonged standing in hot environments; close outpatient follow-up
data.rationale: This is classic vasovagal syncope: a young healthy woman, trigger (prolonged standing, hot environment), prodrome (pallor, diaphoresis), brief LOC (<60 sec), rapid full recovery, no palpitations, normal ECG, and no high-risk features. Vasovagal syncope can be diagnosed clinically with confidence when the story is classic, physical exam is normal, and ECG is normal. The treatment is patient education (counterpressure maneuvers, increased fluid/salt intake, avoid triggers), not hospitalization or urgent cardiology referral. Admission (A) is not indicated for uncomplicated vasovagal — would add cost without benefit. Cardiology referral (B) is reserved for high-risk or unexplained syncope, not classic vasovagal. Holter monitor (D) is used for recurrent unexplained syncope to detect arrhythmia — not appropriate as first-line for classic vasovagal.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Admission is not warranted for classic, low-risk vasovagal syncope — this is evidence-based and cost-effective.
incorrectAnswerRationale.B: Cardiology referral is reserved for high-risk or unexplained syncope — classic vasovagal does not require specialist referral.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Holter monitor and midodrine are not first-line for initial uncomplicated vasovagal syncope.

data.stem: Which clinical feature is MOST predictive of a CARDIAC rather than vasovagal cause of syncope?
options: The event was triggered by emotional stress and occurred while the patient was standing
The patient reports diaphoresis and nausea 30 seconds before losing consciousness
The episode occurred without warning while the patient was seated, and recovery took 10 minutes
The patient is 22 years old with a prior history of vasovagal syncope and had one episode after blood draw
correctAnswer: The episode occurred without warning while the patient was seated, and recovery took 10 minutes
data.rationale: Cardiac syncope is characterized by: (1) NO prodrome or extremely brief prodrome, (2) syncope while seated or supine (not positional/standing-related), (3) prolonged recovery period. A 10-minute recovery (prolonged LOC) and absence of warning while seated are distinctly cardiac features. Vasovagal syncope almost always has a prodromal phase (diaphoresis, nausea, lightheadedness), occurs in upright position, and has very rapid recovery (<1-2 min). Emotional stress trigger and standing position (A) and diaphoresis/nausea prodrome (B) are classic vasovagal features. Prior vasovagal history with a situational trigger (blood draw) (D) is also classic vasovagal.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Emotional trigger + upright posture = vasovagal predictors.
incorrectAnswerRationale.B: Diaphoresis and nausea prodrome = classic vasovagal features.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Situational trigger (blood draw) + prior vasovagal history = vasovagal syncope.

data.stem: A 74-year-old man on metoprolol 50 mg BID, lisinopril 10 mg, furosemide 20 mg, and tamsulosin 0.4 mg reports two episodes of lightheadedness and near-fainting when rising from a chair over the past 2 weeks. He feels completely normal when seated. Which is the MOST likely diagnosis and first management step?
options: Vasovagal syncope — perform tilt table testing
Orthostatic hypotension from polypharmacy — check orthostatic vital signs; review and adjust medications
Arrhythmic syncope — order 30-day cardiac event monitor
Carotid sinus hypersensitivity — perform carotid sinus massage
correctAnswer: Orthostatic hypotension from polypharmacy — check orthostatic vital signs; review and adjust medications
data.rationale: This elderly patient on four medications with known orthostatic properties (beta-blocker, ACE inhibitor, loop diuretic, alpha-blocker for BPH) has classic medication-induced orthostatic hypotension: symptoms only on rising, completely well when seated. Orthostatic vital signs should be measured (supine → standing BP at 1, 2, 3 minutes). Management includes medication review: tamsulosin (alpha-blocker) is a common culprit; furosemide contributes volume depletion; metoprolol blunts compensatory tachycardia on standing. Address dehydration, review timing and dosing of antihypertensives. Vasovagal (A) presents with triggers and prodrome in typically younger patients — not a position-specific trigger without prodrome. Arrhythmic syncope (C) is position-independent. Carotid sinus massage (D) is performed for carotid sinus hypersensitivity (syncope with neck turning/collar pressure in older men) — not applicable here.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Vasovagal syncope features are absent — no emotional trigger, no prodrome; the position-specific trigger strongly suggests orthostasis.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: Arrhythmic syncope is position-independent; event monitor is not the first step before addressing obvious pharmacologic cause.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Carotid sinus massage is not indicated here; clinical picture is medication-induced orthostasis.

data.stem: A patient presents to urgent care after a syncopal episode. Which piece of history is the MOST important in distinguishing seizure from syncope?
options: The patient was incontinent during the episode
The episode lasted approximately 60 seconds
The patient was confused and disoriented for 20 minutes after regaining consciousness
The patient's leg jerked during the episode
correctAnswer: The patient was confused and disoriented for 20 minutes after regaining consciousness
data.rationale: Postictal confusion lasting minutes to an hour is the most discriminating feature of seizure versus syncope. After a syncopal episode, recovery is rapid and complete (seconds to 1-2 minutes with essentially full orientation). A 20-minute period of confusion and disorientation strongly suggests seizure with postictal state. Incontinence (A) can occur in both seizure and syncope — it is not discriminating. Duration of 60 seconds (B) can occur in both conditions. Brief myoclonic jerks (D) can occur in syncope (anoxic seizure — brief oxygen deprivation causes muscle twitches) and should NOT be over-interpreted as seizure — the duration matters: >30-60 seconds of rhythmic clonic movements = seizure; brief (<10 sec) myoclonic jerks after collapse = can be anoxic syncope.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Incontinence occurs in both syncope and seizure — not discriminating.
incorrectAnswerRationale.B: Duration alone does not distinguish seizure from syncope.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Brief myoclonic jerks during syncope (anoxic myoclonus) are common and should not be misinterpreted as epileptic seizure.

data.stem: A 52-year-old man presents to urgent care with BP 196/118 and severe headache for 2 hours. He stopped his lisinopril 3 weeks ago because he ran out. He is alert and oriented. Fundoscopic exam is attempted but cannot be completed. There is no focal neurological deficit, no chest pain, no dyspnea. Which is the MOST appropriate next step?
options: Administer sublingual nifedipine to rapidly lower BP
Call 911 for emergency transport — all BP >180/120 requires hospital admission
Have the patient rest for 20-30 minutes; re-check BP; resume lisinopril; arrange 24-48 hour follow-up if repeat BP is improved and exam remains normal
Start IV labetalol in urgent care to rapidly reduce MAP by 30% in 1 hour
correctAnswer: Have the patient rest for 20-30 minutes; re-check BP; resume lisinopril; arrange 24-48 hour follow-up if repeat BP is improved and exam remains normal
data.rationale: This patient meets criteria for HYPERTENSIVE URGENCY: BP ≥180/120 WITHOUT evidence of acute end-organ damage. He is alert, neurologically intact, has no chest pain, no dyspnea, and no fundoscopic changes identified. The headache likely reflects severely elevated BP without encephalopathy (no confusion, no vomiting, no visual changes, no focal deficit). Appropriate management of urgency: rest in a quiet room for 20-30 minutes (white coat effect can account for a significant portion of the elevation), restart/resume his antihypertensive (lisinopril), and arrange close outpatient follow-up within 24-48 hours. Do NOT administer sublingual nifedipine (A) — this is specifically dangerous and is associated with precipitating MI and stroke by causing uncontrolled rapid BP drop. Hospital admission (B) is not required for urgency without end-organ damage. IV labetalol (D) is for hypertensive emergency requiring IV management — not outpatient urgency.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Sublingual nifedipine is dangerous — rapid uncontrolled BP drop can precipitate stroke and MI; it is contraindicated.
incorrectAnswerRationale.B: Hypertensive urgency (without end-organ damage) does not mandate hospital admission — oral agents and close follow-up are appropriate.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: IV antihypertensives are for hypertensive emergency with end-organ damage — not urgency in an alert, neurologically intact patient.

data.stem: A 44-year-old woman with known hypertension presents with BP 210/125, sudden onset severe ripping chest pain radiating to the back, and a 20 mmHg BP difference between right (210/125) and left (190/115) arms. Which diagnosis is MOST likely, and what is the CORRECT initial management?
options: Hypertensive urgency — administer oral amlodipine 10 mg and recheck in 2 hours
Acute MI — give sublingual nitroglycerin and aspirin; call 911
Aortic dissection — call 911 IMMEDIATELY; target SBP <120 mmHg with IV beta-blocker + vasodilator to reduce shear force
Hypertensive emergency with cardiac involvement — start oral labetalol 100 mg
correctAnswer: Aortic dissection — call 911 IMMEDIATELY; target SBP <120 mmHg with IV beta-blocker + vasodilator to reduce shear force
data.rationale: The combination of: ripping/tearing chest pain radiating to the back, significantly elevated BP, and inter-arm BP differential (>20 mmHg systolic) is the classic presentation of aortic dissection — a vascular catastrophe requiring immediate surgical evaluation. Call 911. Management targets: reduce SBP to <120 mmHg AND heart rate <60 bpm to reduce arterial wall shear force. IV beta-blocker (esmolol, labetalol) is first-line (controls both BP and HR); add sodium nitroprusside or nicardipine as vasodilator only AFTER beta-blockade (pure vasodilators can cause reflex tachycardia that worsens shear force). Oral medications (A, D) are completely inadequate for this vascular emergency. Aspirin and nitroglycerin (B) could worsen outcomes: aspirin's antiplatelet effects are harmful in dissection; nitroglycerin without HR control is dangerous.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Aortic dissection is a vascular emergency requiring immediate IV management and surgical evaluation — oral medications are contraindicated.
incorrectAnswerRationale.B: While aortic dissection can mimic MI, aspirin (antiplatelet) is harmful if dissection has occurred; nitroglycerin without HR control worsens dissection.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Oral labetalol is not appropriate for aortic dissection — IV medication and emergent imaging are required.

data.stem: A 31-year-old woman at 36 weeks gestation presents with BP 168/110, severe headache, and right upper quadrant pain. Urine protein is 3+ on dipstick. Labs show elevated AST/ALT and thrombocytopenia (platelets 78,000). Which condition is MOST likely, and what is the immediate priority?
options: Hypertensive urgency in pregnancy — administer oral labetalol and discharge
Severe preeclampsia with HELLP syndrome — call obstetrics/911 immediately; magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis; IV antihypertensive
Gestational hypertension — recheck BP in 1 week; no intervention needed
Chronic hypertension exacerbation — adjust methyldopa dose
correctAnswer: Severe preeclampsia with HELLP syndrome — call obstetrics/911 immediately; magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis; IV antihypertensive
data.rationale: This patient has HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets) complicating severe preeclampsia: BP ≥160/110, severe headache, RUQ pain (liver capsule distension), elevated transaminases, and thrombocytopenia. This is an obstetric emergency with risk of eclampsia (seizure), liver rupture, DIC, and placental abruption. Immediate actions: (1) Call obstetrics and 911, (2) magnesium sulfate IV for seizure prophylaxis (also treats/prevents eclampsia), (3) IV antihypertensive (labetalol or hydralazine) to reduce BP, (4) stabilize for delivery — delivery is the definitive treatment. Oral labetalol discharge (A) is dangerously inadequate for BP ≥160/110 with HELLP. 'Gestational hypertension' (C) is below the severity threshold — BP ≥160/110 + HELLP = severe preeclampsia. Adjusting methyldopa (D) is completely insufficient.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: BP ≥160/110 + HELLP criteria is not urgency — this is a maternal emergency requiring immediate hospitalization.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: HELLP syndrome with BP ≥160/110 and proteinuria is not gestational hypertension — it is severe preeclampsia with life-threatening complications.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Oral medication adjustment is inadequate for HELLP syndrome — IV treatment and delivery are required.

data.stem: A 68-year-old man has blood pressure measured at 184/110 and 188/112 on two occasions in the office today. He has no symptoms. Review of his medications reveals he takes amlodipine 10 mg, lisinopril 40 mg, and hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg — all at maximum doses. He is compliant with medications. Spironolactone has not been tried. What is the MOST appropriate next action?
options: Diagnose resistant hypertension; add spironolactone 25-50 mg daily; workup for secondary hypertension causes
Refer to ED for hypertensive emergency management
Double the dose of lisinopril to 80 mg daily
Discharge and advise low-sodium diet as the only needed adjustment
correctAnswer: Diagnose resistant hypertension; add spironolactone 25-50 mg daily; workup for secondary hypertension causes
data.rationale: Resistant hypertension is defined as BP above goal despite ≥3 antihypertensive agents at maximum tolerated doses including a diuretic (with confirmed adherence). This patient meets all criteria: on 3 agents at max dose, compliant, still uncontrolled. Next step: (1) add spironolactone 25-50 mg/day — primary hyperaldosteronism is highly prevalent in resistant HTN and spironolactone is the most effective add-on regardless of aldosterone status, (2) workup for secondary causes (serum aldosterone/plasma renin activity, renal ultrasound for RAS, TSH, sleep study for OSA). ED referral (B) is not indicated — this is not a hypertensive emergency (no end-organ damage; the BP elevation is chronic, not acute). Lisinopril cannot be doubled to 80 mg (C) — 40 mg is the maximum recommended dose. Diet alone (D) is insufficient for resistant hypertension.

incorrectAnswerRationale.B: No end-organ damage and no acute severity — not a hypertensive emergency; resistant HTN management in outpatient setting.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: 40 mg is the maximum recommended dose of lisinopril; doubling is not evidence-based.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Dietary advice alone is insufficient when medications at max doses have failed to control BP.

data.stem: A 77-year-old woman on amlodipine 10 mg and losartan 100 mg has SBP consistently 148-156 mmHg over the past 3 months. DBP is 68-72 mmHg. She reports dizziness on standing. Orthostatic vitals show a drop from SBP 150 to 118 when standing for 2 minutes. Which is the MOST appropriate management?
options: Add a third antihypertensive to achieve SBP <130
Reduce antihypertensive therapy; address orthostatic hypotension; target SBP 130-140 for her age
Switch to a beta-blocker — more effective for isolated systolic hypertension
Refer to cardiology for implantable device to stabilize blood pressure
correctAnswer: Reduce antihypertensive therapy; address orthostatic hypotension; target SBP 130-140 for her age
data.rationale: This older woman has two problems: (1) incompletely controlled isolated systolic HTN (SBP 148-156), and (2) significant orthostatic hypotension (SBP drop of >30 mmHg on standing) — a fall risk. In frail elderly patients, the risk of orthostatic hypotension causing falls often outweighs the benefit of tighter systolic control. Current guidelines recommend SBP target 130-140 in elderly patients without frailty; in frail elderly with symptomatic orthostasis, a less stringent target (140-150) may be appropriate. Reducing one antihypertensive (consider reducing amlodipine or losartan dose) should be the first step to address the orthostatic hypotension. Adding a third agent (A) when orthostasis is already causing symptoms would worsen fall risk significantly. Beta-blockers (C) are not preferred for isolated systolic HTN in the elderly. Cardiology implantable device (D) has no role in HTN management.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Adding more antihypertensives when symptomatic orthostasis already exists will worsen fall risk — inappropriate.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: Beta-blockers are not the preferred class for isolated systolic HTN in the elderly and would worsen orthostasis.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: No such device exists for hypertension management in this context.

data.stem: A 45-year-old woman with hypertension controlled on lisinopril presents with BP 188/116 and new onset confusion, headache, and blurry vision. You perform fundoscopy and observe bilateral papilledema with flame hemorrhages. Creatinine has risen from 1.1 to 2.3 mg/dL since last month. What is the CORRECT classification and next step?
options: Hypertensive urgency — adjust oral medications and recheck in 48 hours
Hypertensive emergency with evidence of end-organ damage (brain, kidney, eye) — transfer to ED immediately
Chronic hypertensive nephropathy — schedule nephrology referral within 2 weeks
White coat hypertension — order ambulatory blood pressure monitoring
correctAnswer: Hypertensive emergency with evidence of end-organ damage (brain, kidney, eye) — transfer to ED immediately
data.rationale: This patient has HYPERTENSIVE EMERGENCY: BP ≥180/120 WITH multiple simultaneous end-organ damage manifestations: hypertensive encephalopathy (confusion + headache), hypertensive retinopathy (papilledema + flame hemorrhages), and hypertensive nephropathy (acute creatinine doubling). This is a medical emergency requiring immediate ED transfer for IV antihypertensive therapy with controlled reduction (reduce MAP by 10-15% in first hour, 15-25% over next 23 hours — NOT a rapid normalization). Visual changes with papilledema and acute kidney injury clearly exclude the diagnosis of urgency. Nephrology referral in 2 weeks (C) is dangerously delayed for acute creatinine doubling with hypertensive crisis. Ambulatory BP monitoring (D) is used to diagnose white coat hypertension — inappropriate when the patient has active end-organ damage.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: End-organ damage (confusion, papilledema, acute kidney injury) converts this from urgency to emergency — not manageable with oral agents outpatient.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: Acute creatinine doubling with BP crisis requires emergency management, not routine nephrology referral.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Active end-organ damage rules out white coat hypertension; ABPM is irrelevant in this emergency.

data.stem: A 58-year-old man presents with a 2-month history of progressive difficulty swallowing solids (now also liquids), 18-pound unintentional weight loss, and no heartburn. He smokes 1 PPD and drinks 2 glasses of wine nightly. Which combination of ALARM symptoms and workup is MOST appropriate?
options: Diagnose GERD empirically; start omeprazole 20 mg daily and reassess in 8 weeks
Prescribe soft diet and refer to speech therapy for dysphagia evaluation
Refer urgently for upper endoscopy (EGD) within 2 weeks — progressive dysphagia + weight loss meets ALARM criteria for upper GI malignancy
Order a barium swallow — safest initial imaging for dysphagia
correctAnswer: Refer urgently for upper endoscopy (EGD) within 2 weeks — progressive dysphagia + weight loss meets ALARM criteria for upper GI malignancy
data.rationale: This patient has multiple ALARM (red flag) features for esophageal or gastric malignancy: progressive dysphagia (from solids to liquids = obstructive lesion advancing), significant weight loss (18 lbs), and risk factors (smoking, alcohol, male, age >50). ALARM criteria mandate upper endoscopy (EGD) without delay — empiric acid suppression without endoscopy in this setting risks delaying a cancer diagnosis. The NICE NG12 guideline recommends urgent EGD within 2 weeks for patients with dysphagia or dyspepsia with red flags. Starting omeprazole without EGD (A) would delay diagnosis — acceptable only for uncomplicated dyspepsia without red flags. Speech therapy (B) is appropriate for neurological dysphagia (oropharyngeal, stroke-related) — not for obstructive structural lesion. Barium swallow (D) can identify esophageal lesions but does not allow biopsy — EGD is required for definitive diagnosis.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: ALARM symptoms prohibit empiric acid suppression without endoscopy — dysphagia + weight loss = urgent workup.
incorrectAnswerRationale.B: Speech therapy is for neurological/oropharyngeal dysphagia, not progressive esophageal obstruction.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Barium swallow cannot provide tissue diagnosis — EGD with biopsy is required for suspected esophageal malignancy.

data.stem: A 63-year-old man presents with 3 episodes of gross (visible) hematuria over 2 months, each painless and self-resolving. He is a former smoker. Physical exam including genital exam is unremarkable. Urinalysis today shows no blood (done 2 weeks after last episode). What is the MOST appropriate next step?
options: Reassure — urinalysis today is negative; he does not have hematuria
Diagnose benign prostatic hyperplasia — common in his age group and can cause hematuria
Refer to urology for cystoscopy and CT urogram — visible hematuria in an adult ≥35 requires workup regardless of current UA result
Prescribe tamsulosin for BPH and reassess in 3 months
correctAnswer: Refer to urology for cystoscopy and CT urogram — visible hematuria in an adult ≥35 requires workup regardless of current UA result
data.rationale: Gross (visible) hematuria in an adult ≥35 is an ABSOLUTE indication for urology referral and cystoscopy + CT urogram, REGARDLESS of whether subsequent urinalysis is negative. Bladder cancer and renal cell carcinoma commonly present with intermittent painless hematuria. A negative UA when the patient is not currently bleeding does not exclude a lesion — the episodes can be episodic. The AUA recommends cystoscopy for all adults ≥35 with gross hematuria (even single episode) or microscopic hematuria without explanation. BPH can cause hematuria but is a diagnosis of exclusion — urological malignancy must be ruled out first. Tamsulosin for BPH (D) without ruling out malignancy first is inappropriate.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Three prior episodes of gross hematuria constitute a clinical history that requires workup — a negative UA at a different time does not negate this.
incorrectAnswerRationale.B: BPH is a diagnosis of exclusion — bladder cancer and RCC must be excluded first in a male smoker >60 with gross hematuria.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: BPH treatment without ruling out malignancy in a symptomatic gross hematuria case is inappropriate — workup first.

data.stem: A 71-year-old woman presents with 3 months of generalized fatigue, 12-pound weight loss, and night sweats requiring change of nightclothes. She has no fever. On exam, you note a 2 cm firm, non-tender left supraclavicular lymph node. Which statement is MOST accurate?
options: Supraclavicular lymphadenopathy in an elderly patient is commonly reactive; observe for 8 weeks before workup
A supraclavicular lymph node + B-symptoms (weight loss, night sweats, fever) suggests lymphoma or other malignancy requiring urgent workup
Order a course of antibiotics first — infectious lymphadenopathy must be excluded before malignancy
Reassure — night sweats are common in postmenopausal women and the weight loss is modest
correctAnswer: A supraclavicular lymph node + B-symptoms (weight loss, night sweats, fever) suggests lymphoma or other malignancy requiring urgent workup
data.rationale: Supraclavicular lymphadenopathy (Virchow's node on the left) is ALWAYS concerning for malignancy — it represents drainage from the thoracic and abdominal organs and is highly associated with lymphoma, GI, lung, and breast malignancy. Combined with B-symptoms (weight loss >10%, drenching night sweats, ± fever) — this pattern demands urgent cancer evaluation. Workup: CBC with differential, comprehensive metabolic panel, LDH (elevated in lymphoma), uric acid, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, HIV, and hematology/oncology referral. 'Observe for 8 weeks' (A) is wrong — supraclavicular nodes are never watched; they are worked up urgently. Empiric antibiotics (C) delay diagnosis — there is no infectious context here. Normalizing night sweats as menopausal (D) misses the B-symptom triad context and concurrent supraclavicular adenopathy.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Supraclavicular lymphadenopathy is NEVER observed without workup — it is high-risk by definition.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: Antibiotics are not appropriate without evidence of infection; empiric antibiotics delay malignancy diagnosis.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Night sweats + weight loss + supraclavicular node = B-symptoms suggesting malignancy; cannot be attributed to menopause.

data.stem: A 49-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with a palpable right breast mass she noticed 3 weeks ago. It is 2 cm, firm, non-tender, and non-mobile on exam. She has no nipple discharge, no skin changes. Her last mammogram 2 years ago was normal. Which is the MOST appropriate next step?
options: Repeat mammogram in 1 year — prior normal mammogram reduces cancer risk
Prescribe evening primrose oil and reassure — breast pain and masses are common in postmenopausal women
Order diagnostic mammogram + breast ultrasound; refer to surgery or breast specialist for evaluation
Observe for 6 weeks — if the mass persists, then order imaging
correctAnswer: Order diagnostic mammogram + breast ultrasound; refer to surgery or breast specialist for evaluation
data.rationale: A palpable breast mass in a postmenopausal woman requires imaging and tissue diagnosis — a prior normal mammogram does NOT eliminate cancer risk and is not reassuring in the face of a NEW palpable abnormality. Diagnostic mammogram (not screening) + breast ultrasound should be ordered urgently. Refer to breast surgery or breast specialist for evaluation, which may include core needle biopsy based on imaging findings. In postmenopausal women, any new palpable mass has significantly higher malignancy risk than in premenopausal women. A 6-week observation period (D) is inappropriate for a new palpable breast mass in a postmenopausal woman — delayed diagnosis of breast cancer worsens prognosis. Options A and B are dangerously inadequate.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: A prior normal mammogram does not negate the clinical significance of a new palpable mass — diagnostic imaging is required.
incorrectAnswerRationale.B: Evening primrose oil and reassurance are not appropriate for a new palpable breast mass in a postmenopausal woman.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: 6-week observation is not appropriate for a firm, non-mobile mass in a postmenopausal patient — immediate imaging is required.

data.stem: A 65-year-old woman with a 40 pack-year smoking history presents for her annual visit. She has no respiratory symptoms and is in good health. She quit smoking 3 years ago. She asks about lung cancer screening. Which recommendation is MOST consistent with current USPSTF guidelines?
options: No screening recommended — she quit 3 years ago and is no longer high-risk
Annual low-dose CT (LDCT) scan of the chest — she meets criteria: age 50-80, 20+ pack-year history, current smoker OR quit within the past 15 years
Annual chest X-ray — adequate for lung cancer screening in former smokers
Sputum cytology every 2 years — appropriate screening in symptomatic smokers
correctAnswer: Annual low-dose CT (LDCT) scan of the chest — she meets criteria: age 50-80, 20+ pack-year history, current smoker OR quit within the past 15 years
data.rationale: USPSTF 2021 recommendation: annual low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening for adults aged 50-80 who have a 20 pack-year smoking history AND currently smoke OR quit within the past 15 years. This patient: age 65, 40 pack-year history, quit 3 years ago (within 15 years) — she meets ALL criteria. Screening can stop when: age >80, >15 years since quitting, or health problems limiting treatment or life expectancy. Screening stops at 15 years post-cessation (A is incorrect — 3 years does not disqualify). Chest X-ray (C) has NO proven mortality benefit for lung cancer screening and is NOT recommended. Sputum cytology (D) is not recommended — insufficient sensitivity and not a USPSTF-endorsed strategy.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: She quit only 3 years ago — screening continues for 15 years after cessation; she remains a screening candidate.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: Chest X-ray has no mortality benefit for lung cancer screening — LDCT is the only recommended modality.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Sputum cytology is not a recommended screening strategy for lung cancer.

data.stem: A 28-year-old man who has sex with men (MSM) presents requesting PrEP. He reports condomless anal sex with 2-3 partners per month. He had a gonorrhea diagnosis 4 months ago. His most recent HIV test was 6 months ago (negative). Which is the MOST appropriate first step before prescribing PrEP?
options: Prescribe Truvada based on the negative HIV test from 6 months ago
Order a 4th-generation HIV antigen/antibody test (or HIV RNA); withhold PrEP until result is confirmed negative within the past 7 days
Start PrEP and simultaneously treat presumed gonorrhea — the prior infection is a contraindication
Refer to infectious disease before prescribing PrEP as a first-time prescriber
correctAnswer: Order a 4th-generation HIV antigen/antibody test (or HIV RNA); withhold PrEP until result is confirmed negative within the past 7 days
data.rationale: Before prescribing PrEP, a RECENT negative HIV test (within 7 days of initiation) is mandatory. An HIV test from 6 months ago is not adequate — the patient may have acquired HIV in the interim (he has risk behaviors and had bacterial STI 4 months ago, suggesting ongoing exposure). Starting PrEP in an HIV-positive individual risks developing drug resistance to tenofovir and emtricitabine, the backbones of most ART regimens. The gold standard pre-PrEP HIV test: 4th-generation HIV-1/2 antigen/antibody assay. Prescribing based on a 6-month-old test (A) is medically inappropriate. Prior gonorrhea (C) is not a contraindication — in fact it is an indicator of higher risk that strengthens PrEP eligibility. Infectious disease referral (D) is not required for PrEP initiation — NPs are authorized and expected to prescribe PrEP independently.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: A 6-month-old HIV test is inadequate — must confirm HIV-negative status within 7 days of starting PrEP.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: Prior gonorrhea is an eligibility indicator for PrEP, not a contraindication. Prior STI increases urgency for PrEP.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: NPs are fully qualified to prescribe PrEP without specialist referral — this is core NP competency.

data.stem: A 34-year-old transgender woman (assigned male at birth, currently on estrogen therapy) requests PrEP. She has receptive anal sex with multiple cisgender male partners and reports no current condom use. Which PrEP regimen is MOST appropriate, and which monitoring schedule is REQUIRED?
options: TAF/FTC (Descovy) — approved for transgender women; HIV test annually
TDF/FTC (Truvada) OR TAF/FTC (Descovy) — both approved for transgender women and MSM; HIV test every 3 months; STI screening every 3-6 months; renal function at baseline and q6 months for Truvada
TAF/FTC only — Truvada is contraindicated with estrogen due to drug interactions
Injectable cabotegravir only — oral PrEP is not approved for transgender women
correctAnswer: TDF/FTC (Truvada) OR TAF/FTC (Descovy) — both approved for transgender women and MSM; HIV test every 3 months; STI screening every 3-6 months; renal function at baseline and q6 months for Truvada
data.rationale: Both TDF/FTC (Truvada) and TAF/FTC (Descovy) are FDA-approved for PrEP in transgender women for receptive anal sex. There are NO clinically significant drug-drug interactions between either TDF/FTC or TAF/FTC and gender-affirming estrogen therapy. Monitoring requirements are the same for any PrEP regimen: HIV test every 3 months (mandatory), STI screening every 3-6 months (anatomic site-specific NAAT), and for Truvada specifically — renal function (CrCl) every 6 months due to TDF's tubular toxicity. TAF has better renal and bone safety. Injectable cabotegravir (C) is also approved and an excellent option for transgender women (HPTN 083 trial included them) but is not the ONLY option — oral PrEP is fully appropriate. Truvada is not contraindicated with estrogen (C — incorrect statement).

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: HIV test annually is wrong — mandatory HIV testing every 3 months for all PrEP users.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: There are no significant drug interactions between Truvada and estrogen therapy — this statement is incorrect.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Oral PrEP is approved and appropriate for transgender women — injectable is an option, not the only option.

data.stem: A patient has been on TDF/FTC (Truvada) PrEP for 8 months. At today's 3-month monitoring visit, his HIV antigen/antibody test returns REACTIVE. HIV RNA PCR is sent and returns at 22,000 copies/mL. What is the MOST appropriate immediate next step?
options: Continue Truvada — 22,000 copies/mL is within the expected PrEP drug-level fluctuation range
Stop Truvada immediately; urgent referral to HIV specialist within days for drug resistance testing and ART initiation; do not start new antiretrovirals until resistance profile known
Switch to TAF/FTC (Descovy) — better efficacy in breakthrough infection
Add a third antiretroviral (dolutegravir) to the current TDF/FTC to create a full treatment regimen
correctAnswer: Stop Truvada immediately; urgent referral to HIV specialist within days for drug resistance testing and ART initiation; do not start new antiretrovirals until resistance profile known
data.rationale: HIV seroconversion while on PrEP represents a PrEP failure and requires immediate and careful management. The critical danger: TDF/FTC alone is NOT a complete ART regimen — it is a 2-drug backbone. If TDF/FTC is continued as the only antivirals when HIV-positive, the virus will develop resistance to tenofovir and emtricitabine (M184V and K65R mutations), severely limiting future ART options. Actions: (1) STOP Truvada immediately, (2) send resistance testing (genotype) on the CURRENT sample before starting any ART — viral fitness is higher when treatment naive, (3) urgent HIV specialist referral within days, (4) do NOT add empiric dolutegravir without knowing the resistance profile — timing of ART initiation should be guided by specialist. Switching to TAF/FTC (C) is equally inappropriate — it provides the same 2-drug backbone inadequate for HIV treatment. Adding dolutegravir (D) without resistance testing is premature.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: 22,000 copies/mL confirms active HIV infection, not drug-level fluctuation — PrEP failure is occurring.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: TAF/FTC has the same active drugs as Truvada — switching provides no benefit; still an incomplete HIV treatment regimen.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Adding antiretrovirals without knowing the resistance profile can select for resistance — specialist guidance is required first.

data.stem: A 26-year-old MSM on TDF/FTC PrEP reports that he sometimes misses doses when he is traveling for work. He asks about the minimum number of days he needs to take Truvada before anal sex to be protected. What is the CORRECT answer?
options: 1 day — TDF/FTC protects immediately after the first dose
3 days — TDF/FTC reaches protective levels in rectal tissue within 72 hours
7 days — consistent daily dosing for at least 7 days is required for full protection with rectal exposure in MSM
21 days — the protection timeline is the same for all exposure types
correctAnswer: 7 days — consistent daily dosing for at least 7 days is required for full protection with rectal exposure in MSM
data.rationale: The time to protection varies by exposure type for TDF/FTC: Rectal exposure (insertive or receptive anal sex): 7 days of consistent daily dosing required for full protection. Vaginal exposure: 21 days. Injection drug use: 21 days. This difference reflects tissue concentration kinetics — rectal tissue achieves protective drug levels faster than cervicovaginal tissue. For MSM with rectal exposure, 7 days is the minimum. Missing doses below this threshold may not provide adequate protection. 1 day (A) is incorrect — TDF needs time to accumulate in rectal tissue. 3 days (B) is insufficient — 7 days is the established minimum for rectal. 21 days (D) applies to vaginal and injection exposure, not rectal.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: TDF/FTC does not provide immediate protection — it requires time to reach protective tissue concentrations.
incorrectAnswerRationale.B: 3 days is insufficient for rectal protection — 7 days is required.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: 21 days applies to vaginal tissue and injection exposure, not rectal exposure in MSM.

data.stem: A 32-year-old cisgender woman with CrCl 52 mL/min requests PrEP for protection during vaginal sex with her HIV-positive partner (who is on ART but has an unknown viral load). Which PrEP regimen is MOST appropriate for this patient?
options: TAF/FTC (Descovy) — superior for vaginal exposure and better renal safety than Truvada
TDF/FTC (Truvada) — approved for all eligible individuals including cisgender women; renal monitoring required given CrCl 52
Injectable cabotegravir (Apretude) — preferred for all serodiscordant couples
No PrEP indicated — serodiscordant couple where partner is on ART has near-zero risk
correctAnswer: TDF/FTC (Truvada) — approved for all eligible individuals including cisgender women; renal monitoring required given CrCl 52
data.rationale: For cisgender women, TDF/FTC (Truvada) is the only FDA-approved oral PrEP — TAF/FTC (Descovy) is NOT approved for cisgender women or transgender men for vaginal exposure (not studied in clinical trials for vaginal exposure). With CrCl 52, Truvada can still be prescribed but requires careful renal monitoring (eGFR every 6 months; consider switching to injectable cabotegravir if renal function declines further). Descovy (A) is NOT approved for vaginal exposure — this is a critical prescribing distinction. Injectable cabotegravir (C) is approved for cisgender women (HPTN 084 trial) and is excellent for this patient, but it requires clinic visits every 2 months and is not universally preferred in all serodiscordant couples. Option D is incorrect — while U=U (Undetectable = Untransmittable) is real when viral load is confirmed undetectable, the partner's viral load is UNKNOWN here. PrEP is appropriate until viral suppression is confirmed and maintained.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Descovy is NOT FDA-approved for vaginal exposure in cisgender women or transgender men — this is a critical regulatory distinction.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: Injectable cabotegravir is an option but not universally preferred; Truvada is appropriate for this patient.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: U=U applies when viral load is confirmed undetectable — 'unknown viral load' does not constitute adequate protection.

data.stem: A 24-year-old MSM patient on TDF/FTC PrEP mentions he has been using HIV PrEP for 10 months and has never been tested for anal cancer or received an anal Pap. He has had multiple anal sexual partners. What is the MOST evidence-informed approach regarding anal cancer screening?
options: Anal Pap smear is not recommended unless the patient is HIV-positive
Anal Pap is not validated — no routine screening is recommended for any MSM population

Offer anal Pap cytology — MSM (especially HIV-positive MSM) have significantly elevated anal HPV exposure; the ACS and IDSA recommend consideration for HIV-positive MSM; extend this discussion to HIV-negative MSM with multiple partners as clinical context
Recommend anal Pap annually for ALL adults regardless of sexual orientation
correctAnswer: Offer anal Pap cytology — MSM (especially HIV-positive MSM) have significantly elevated anal HPV exposure; the ACS and IDSA recommend consideration for HIV-positive MSM; extend this discussion to HIV-negative MSM with multiple partners as clinical context
data.rationale: Anal cancer screening via anal Pap cytology is a nuanced area: the strongest evidence and most widely endorsed recommendations apply to HIV-positive MSM (prevalence of anal HSIL and cancer dramatically elevated). For HIV-negative MSM with multiple partners, evidence is growing but guidelines vary by organization. IDSA recommends anal Pap for HIV-positive MSM; the New York State Department of Health and many LGBTQ+ health guidelines extend this to high-risk HIV-negative MSM. Inclusive, culturally competent care means discussing this with the patient and offering it as a clinical option with the caveat that formal universal screening guidelines for HIV-negative MSM are still evolving. Option A is incorrect — it is too restrictive; HIV-negative MSM with extensive HPV exposure benefit from discussion. Option B incorrectly states no screening is recommended. Option D is too broad — anal Pap is not recommended universally for all adults.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Too restrictive — anal HPV exposure is high in HIV-negative MSM with multiple partners; screening discussion is appropriate.
incorrectAnswerRationale.B: Guidelines do recommend anal Pap for HIV-positive MSM — stating no screening is recommended is incorrect.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Anal Pap is not universally recommended for all adults — it targets high-risk populations.

data.stem: A 30-year-old patient who identifies as bisexual expresses discomfort discussing their sexual history with clinic staff. When you meet them, they disclose they have been afraid to ask for PrEP because they feared judgment. What is the MOST appropriate approach as an NP to create an inclusive, effective clinical encounter?
options: Reassure the patient they can trust you; take a sexual history using anatomical and behavioral language (not assumed identities); use the patient's stated pronouns and language
Have the patient complete a standardized heterosexual-oriented sexual history form first, then adapt questions if needed
Ask a colleague to conduct the sexual history because the patient expressed discomfort
Focus only on the PrEP prescription; a full sexual history is not necessary if the patient is requesting PrEP
correctAnswer: Reassure the patient they can trust you; take a sexual history using anatomical and behavioral language (not assumed identities); use the patient's stated pronouns and language
data.rationale: Creating an affirming clinical environment is both an ethical obligation and a clinical requirement for LGBTQ+ patients, who face documented higher rates of healthcare avoidance, stigma, and inadequate care. The correct approach: acknowledge the patient's courage in seeking care, explicitly validate their identity, use anatomical and behavioral language rather than assumed identity-based terminology (ask what types of sex they have and with whom, not 'are you gay?'), use the patient's preferred pronouns and language, and explain that this information guides clinical care without judgment. A full, inclusive sexual history is essential — PrEP eligibility and regimen selection depend on specific anatomical exposures and partner types. Standardized heterosexual-oriented forms (B) create barriers and invalidate LGBTQ+ patients. Delegating to a colleague (C) avoids the clinical responsibility. Skipping the sexual history (D) is medically incorrect — exposure type determines which PrEP regimen is appropriate.

incorrectAnswerRationale.B: Heterosexual-oriented forms are exclusionary and inadequate for LGBTQ+ patients; they communicate implicit bias.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: Delegating avoids the NP's responsibility; it may also feel stigmatizing to the patient.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: The sexual history is clinically necessary for PrEP eligibility confirmation and regimen selection — it cannot be skipped.

data.stem: A 79-year-old woman comes in after her second fall in 3 months. She has hypertension, osteoarthritis, and anxiety. Current medications include amlodipine 10 mg, losartan 50 mg, zolpidem 10 mg nightly, lorazepam 0.5 mg PRN, and ibuprofen 400 mg PRN. She reports difficulty falling asleep. The Timed Up and Go test takes 16 seconds. Which medication change is MOST likely to reduce her fall risk?
options: Switch ibuprofen to celecoxib — less GI toxicity
Discontinue zolpidem and lorazepam; initiate CBT-I and consider low-dose doxepin 3-6 mg if pharmacotherapy needed for sleep
Add mirtazapine for anxiety and insomnia — dual benefit
Add gabapentin 100 mg nightly — effective for sleep and anxiety in older adults
correctAnswer: Discontinue zolpidem and lorazepam; initiate CBT-I and consider low-dose doxepin 3-6 mg if pharmacotherapy needed for sleep
data.rationale: Zolpidem (Z-drug) and lorazepam (benzodiazepine) are BOTH Beers Criteria medications and major fall risk contributors through sedation, psychomotor impairment, and next-morning cognitive slowing. TUG of 16 seconds (≥12 sec = high fall risk) confirms objectively elevated fall risk. The highest priority deprescribing target is the combination of a Z-drug + benzodiazepine. CBT-I is the first-line treatment for insomnia — more effective than pharmacotherapy for chronic insomnia in older adults. If pharmacotherapy is needed, low-dose doxepin 3-6 mg (FDA-approved for sleep maintenance insomnia in older adults) has minimal anticholinergic activity at this dose. Switching to celecoxib (A) is a reasonable GI protection step but doesn't address the fall risk. Mirtazapine (C) is sedating and has anticholinergic and orthostatic properties — worsens fall risk. Gabapentin (D) is a Beers Criteria medication for older adults — increases sedation and fall risk.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Celecoxib reduces GI risk but doesn't address the major fall risk from sedating medications.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: Mirtazapine is sedating, has anticholinergic effects, and causes orthostasis — increases, not decreases, fall risk.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Gabapentin is listed in Beers Criteria for older adults due to dizziness and fall risk — contraproductive here.

data.stem: A 83-year-old man scores 2/5 on the Fried Frailty Criteria (exhaustion and slow gait speed). His daughter asks you about the prognosis implications of this score. Which statement is MOST accurate?
options: A score of 2 means he is 'pre-frail' — he has elevated risk but is not frail; targeted exercise and nutritional intervention may prevent progression to frailty
A score of 2 means he is 'frail' — immediate nursing home placement is indicated
A score of 2 means he is 'robust' — no interventions are needed
Fried Criteria cannot be applied to men — it was validated in women only
correctAnswer: A score of 2 means he is 'pre-frail' — he has elevated risk but is not frail; targeted exercise and nutritional intervention may prevent progression to frailty
data.rationale: Fried Frailty Criteria (5 components: weight loss, exhaustion, weakness, slow gait, low activity): 0 = robust, 1-2 = pre-frail, ≥3 = frail. This patient with 2 criteria is PRE-FRAIL — he has elevated risk for progression to frailty, adverse outcomes, and disability but is not yet frail. Pre-frailty is a critical intervention window: resistance exercise (most evidence-based frailty intervention), nutritional optimization (especially protein intake), and management of specific deficits (treat exhaustion's cause, address gait issues) can prevent or reverse pre-frailty progression. Nursing home placement (B) is not indicated by pre-frailty alone. He is NOT robust (C) — 2 criteria identifies abnormal. Fried Criteria were validated in both men and women in the Cardiovascular Health Study (D is false).

incorrectAnswerRationale.B: Score of 2 = pre-frail, not frail; nursing home placement is not clinically indicated by pre-frailty alone.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: 2 criteria = pre-frail, not robust — intervention is needed.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Fried Criteria were validated in both sexes in the Cardiovascular Health Study (>5,000 participants).

data.stem: An 80-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation on apixaban, hypertension, and moderate dementia (MoCA 17) falls and sustains a scalp laceration but no bone fracture. After wound care, which is the MOST nuanced next step?
options: Stop apixaban permanently — the fall confirms anticoagulation is too dangerous
Continue apixaban without change — risk of stopping exceeds fall risk in AF patients

Engage a structured risk-benefit conversation with patient and family: AF stroke risk (CHA₂DS₂-VASc calculation) vs. documented bleeding risk from fall; consider HASBLED score; optimize fall prevention; individualize decision with goals of care
Switch to aspirin — lower bleeding risk than anticoagulation with similar stroke prevention
correctAnswer: Engage a structured risk-benefit conversation with patient and family: AF stroke risk (CHA₂DS₂-VASc calculation) vs. documented bleeding risk from fall; consider HASBLED score; optimize fall prevention; individualize decision with goals of care
data.rationale: This is one of the highest-stakes prescribing decisions in geriatrics: anticoagulation in a demented older adult with documented falls and AF. There is no algorithmic answer — this requires individualized shared decision-making. Tools: CHA₂DS₂-VASc score (calculate stroke risk per year), HAS-BLED score (assess modifiable bleeding risk factors). Key evidence: in most patients with AF, the absolute stroke risk reduction from anticoagulation EXCEEDS the absolute fall risk (it would take approximately 295 falls/year to equal the harm from one ischemic stroke). However, dementia introduces adherence concerns, and unwitnessed falls that could cause intracranial hemorrhage are of concern. The decision should involve the patient (if decision-making capable), family/healthcare proxy, and goals of care preferences — not made unilaterally. Simply stopping (A) may expose her to stroke. Continuing unchanged (B) without fall prevention optimization is incomplete. Aspirin (D) is NOT equivalent to anticoagulation for AF stroke prevention — it provides significantly less protection and has similar bleeding risk.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Stopping anticoagulation increases stroke risk substantially — cannot be decided unilaterally based on one fall.
incorrectAnswerRationale.B: Optimizing fall prevention and having a goals-of-care conversation is needed — 'continue unchanged' is an incomplete response.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: Aspirin does not adequately prevent AF-related stroke and has comparable bleeding risk — it is not a substitute for anticoagulation in AF.

data.stem: A 76-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and CKD stage 3a (eGFR 52) is on metformin 1000 mg BID, sitagliptin 100 mg daily, lisinopril 10 mg, and atorvastatin 40 mg. During a medication review, which Beers Criteria concern is MOST clinically significant?
options: Atorvastatin — statins should not be used in patients over 75 per Beers Criteria
Sitagliptin — dose requires adjustment for eGFR 52 (should be 50 mg/day at CrCl 30-50)
Metformin — absolutely contraindicated at eGFR <60 and must be stopped immediately
Lisinopril — ACE inhibitors are contraindicated in all diabetics over 70
correctAnswer: Sitagliptin — dose requires adjustment for eGFR 52 (should be 50 mg/day at CrCl 30-50)
data.rationale: Sitagliptin (Januvia) requires renal dose adjustment: 100 mg daily for CrCl ≥50 mL/min; 50 mg daily for CrCl 30-49; 25 mg daily for CrCl <30. With eGFR 52, the patient is approaching the threshold where dose reduction to 50 mg is indicated (CrCl 30-50 = 50 mg). Checking actual CrCl (Cockcroft-Gault using actual body weight) is important in elderly — serum creatinine may not reflect true GFR. Atorvastatin (A) is not a Beers Criteria concern — statins are generally appropriate in older adults with established ASCVD risk; they are Beers Criteria 'use with caution' only in the absence of any ASCVD or CKD. Metformin (C) is contraindicated at eGFR <30, not <60 — at eGFR 52, use with caution and recheck periodically. Lisinopril (D) has NO Beers Criteria restriction in elderly diabetics — it is actually indicated for renal protection in diabetic nephropathy.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Statins are not Beers Criteria to avoid in older adults with established cardiovascular risk — this statement is inaccurate.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: Metformin can be used with caution down to eGFR 30; contraindicated at <30, not <60.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: ACE inhibitors are indicated in diabetics for renal protection — not contraindicated in older adults.

data.stem: A 72-year-old woman with multiple chronic conditions is being treated with amlodipine 10 mg for hypertension. Her daughter calls to report that her mother started taking diphenhydramine (Benadryl) PM nightly 3 weeks ago for sleep, purchased OTC. The daughter reports her mother has developed mild confusion and constipation over the past 2 weeks and had one fall. The most likely explanation is:
options: Early dementia — cognitive decline is expected at her age
Anticholinergic side effects from diphenhydramine — confusion and constipation are classic anticholinergic toxicity in older adults
Calcium channel blocker toxicity from amlodipine — constipation is a common side effect
Drug interaction between amlodipine and diphenhydramine causing excess CNS sedation
correctAnswer: Anticholinergic side effects from diphenhydramine — confusion and constipation are classic anticholinergic toxicity in older adults
data.rationale: This is a classic cascade prescribing/OTC medication toxicity scenario. Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) is a potent anticholinergic antihistamine — it is specifically listed in the Beers Criteria as a drug to AVOID in older adults. Anticholinergic effects in older adults: confusion/delirium (due to reduced central cholinergic reserve with aging), constipation (GI hypomotility), urinary retention, dry mouth, blurred vision, and fall risk. The timeline is perfect — symptoms began 2 weeks into OTC use. The action: stop diphenhydramine immediately; advise CBT-I or low-dose melatonin instead. Amlodipine (C) can cause constipation (calcium channel blocker GI effect) but does not cause confusion — cannot explain the cognitive changes. Dementia (A) does not develop over 2 weeks. There is no significant pharmacokinetic interaction (D) specifically between amlodipine and diphenhydramine causing CNS toxicity.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: 2-week onset rules out new dementia development — this is acute, reversible toxicity.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: Amlodipine can cause constipation but NOT confusion — it does not cross the blood-brain barrier significantly.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: No significant pharmacokinetic drug-drug interaction between amlodipine and diphenhydramine causes additive CNS toxicity.

data.stem: A 84-year-old woman with multiple falls, osteoporosis, and mild cognitive impairment scores a TUG of 18 seconds. She is started on a structured falls prevention program. Which of the following interventions has the STRONGEST evidence for reducing fall frequency in this population?
options: Hip protectors alone
Multi-component exercise program incorporating balance training and lower extremity strengthening
Vitamin D supplementation alone (without exercise)
Cognitive-behavioral therapy for fear of falling alone
correctAnswer: Multi-component exercise program incorporating balance training and lower extremity strengthening
data.rationale: The Cochrane systematic review of fall prevention interventions shows that multi-component exercise programs (combining balance training, gait training, and lower extremity strengthening — e.g., Otago Exercise Program, Tai Chi) have the strongest evidence for reducing fall rate and fall risk in older adults. These programs reduce falls by 23-34% in community-dwelling older adults. Multi-component programs are superior to single-modality interventions. Hip protectors (A) reduce hip fracture risk IF worn consistently, but they do not reduce fall frequency — and compliance is poor. Vitamin D alone (C) is beneficial when deficiency is corrected (improves neuromuscular function) but insufficient as a standalone fall prevention strategy. CBT for fear of falling (D) addresses psychological barriers and can complement exercise but has weaker fall-rate reduction evidence when used alone.

incorrectAnswerRationale.A: Hip protectors reduce fracture severity but NOT fall frequency — different outcome.
incorrectAnswerRationale.C: Vitamin D correction is helpful but insufficient alone — must be combined with exercise.
incorrectAnswerRationale.D: CBT for fear of falling is a useful adjunct but does not have strong fall-rate reduction evidence when used in isolation.

data.stem: You are calculating drug dosing for a 78-year-old man who weighs 62 kg and has a serum creatinine of 1.1 mg/dL. The eGFR from the lab report reads 68 mL/min/1.73m². You need to calculate his actual creatinine clearance for drug dosing. Which calculation is MOST appropriate?
options: Use the eGFR of 68 directly — it is equivalent to CrCl for dosing purposes
Use Cockcroft-Gault equation: [(140-age) × weight(kg)] / [72 × serum creatinine] — gives estimated CrCl appropriate for drug dosing in elderly
Use the MDRD equation — more accurate than Cockcroft-Gault in elderly
Use ideal body weight only, regardless of actual weight
correctAnswer: Use Cockcroft-Gault equation: [(140-age) × weight(kg)] / [72 × serum creatinine] — gives estimated CrCl appropriate for drug dosing in elderly
data.rationale: Drug dosing in older adults requires Cockcroft-Gault (CG) CrCl, NOT eGFR (CKD-EPI or MDRD). This distinction is clinically critical. Why: eGFR is normalized to body surface area (1.73 m²) for CKD staging purposes; it does not reflect actual drug elimination capacity, especially in elderly patients with reduced muscle mass. An elderly patient with a normal serum creatinine but low muscle mass may have a significantly lower actual CrCl than eGFR suggests.
