He also takes lisinopril 10mg and chlorthalidone 12.5mg. He presents for follow-up and his creatinine has risen from 1.2 to 1.7 mg/dL over 3 months. Which cascade is most likely responsible and what is the most appropriate action?
options: CKD is progressing independently; continue naproxen and increase lisinopril for renoprotection
Chlorthalidone is causing volume depletion and AKI; reduce the diuretic dose
NSAID-ACEi-diuretic triple whammy is causing AKI; discontinue naproxen and recheck creatinine
Add omeprazole and continue naproxen; GI protection will prevent progression
correctAnswer: NSAID-ACEi-diuretic triple whammy is causing AKI; discontinue naproxen and recheck creatinine
data.rationale: The 'triple whammy' nephrotoxic combination — NSAID (inhibits prostaglandin-mediated renal afferent arteriolar dilation) + ACEi/ARB (inhibits angiotensin II-mediated efferent constriction) + diuretic (reduces circulating volume) — is the most common cause of reversible AKI in older adults on multiple medications. Naproxen is the identifiable intervention — it was added 6 months ago and the creatinine rise parallels its use. The NSAID must be discontinued. Creatinine will often return to baseline within weeks. Option A is incorrect: continuing naproxen with increased ACEi further compounds the nephrotoxic combination and will worsen AKI. Option B is incorrect: the diuretic is a contributing factor but naproxen is the key precipitant; reducing the diuretic alone without removing the NSAID does not resolve the triple whammy. Option D is incorrect: omeprazole protects the GI tract from NSAID ulceration — it has zero effect on NSAID-induced nephrotoxicity; creatinine rises from renal hemodynamic effects, not GI effects.

data.stem: A 43-year-old man with moderate psoriasis presents reporting that his ring finger and index finger on both hands have become swollen like 'sausages' and his lower back aches in the morning. His skin disease is currently well-controlled on topical therapy. PASI score is 3. Which action is most appropriate?
options: Continue topical therapy; his skin disease is well-controlled and joint symptoms are likely tendinopathy
Increase topical corticosteroid potency to better control underlying inflammation causing joint symptoms
Refer to rheumatology regardless of PASI score; dactylitis and axial symptoms suggest psoriatic arthritis
Start oral methotrexate based on the PASI score alone; it will address both skin and joints
correctAnswer: Refer to rheumatology regardless of PASI score; dactylitis and axial symptoms suggest psoriatic arthritis
data.rationale: Dactylitis (diffuse swelling of entire digits — 'sausage fingers') combined with inflammatory back pain (morning stiffness, worse at rest) in a patient with psoriasis is psoriatic arthritis until proven otherwise. PsA affects 30% of psoriasis patients and can develop independently of skin severity — PASI 3 does not rule out PsA. Rheumatology referral is indicated for any suspected psoriatic arthritis regardless of skin PASI. Option A is incorrect: dismissing joint symptoms as tendinopathy misses PsA — tendinopathy does not cause dactylitis (sausage digits are a highly specific PsA sign). Option B is incorrect: increasing topical therapy addresses only the skin; it has no effect on joint inflammation in PsA, which requires systemic treatment. Option D is incorrect: starting methotrexate without rheumatology evaluation is appropriate in some cases but premature as first action — confirmation of PsA diagnosis, exclusion of other arthropathies, and baseline labs should be established with specialist input.

data.stem: A 7-year-old boy with moderate atopic dermatitis affecting his antecubital fossae and neck presents with his mother. Despite twice-daily hydrocortisone 1% for 8 weeks, the AD has not improved significantly. She asks about a 'stronger cream.' Which management approach is most appropriate?
options: Prescribe triamcinolone 0.1% to all affected areas including the neck
Prescribe tacrolimus 0.03% ointment for face and sensitive areas; upgrade to mid-potency topical CS for trunk and extremity lesions with a consistent emollient regimen
Refer directly for dupilumab initiation; hydrocortisone failure at 8 weeks indicates moderate-severe disease requiring biologics
Switch to oral prednisone 1mg/kg for 2 weeks to rapidly clear the flare
correctAnswer: Prescribe tacrolimus 0.03% ointment for face and sensitive areas; upgrade to mid-potency topical CS for trunk and extremity lesions with a consistent emollient regimen
data.rationale: This child's AD is undertreated with hydrocortisone 1% (low potency, appropriate for face only). Step-up therapy for moderate AD in a child requires: tacrolimus 0.03% ointment (pediatric dose — not 0.1%) for sensitive areas (neck, eyelids, face) where steroids risk skin atrophy; and mid-potency topical corticosteroids (triamcinolone 0.05% or desonide) for trunk and extremity lesions. Consistent emollient use is the foundation. Option A is incorrect: triamcinolone 0.1% (mid-high potency) should not be applied to the neck in children — this area has thin skin with high absorption; risk of atrophy and hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal suppression is significant. Option C is incorrect: dupilumab is indicated for inadequate response to APPROPRIATE topical therapy — hydrocortisone 1% is not adequate, not 'failed appropriate topical treatment'; step up first, then consider biologic. Option D is incorrect: oral corticosteroids cause AD rebound after discontinuation, promote skin barrier impairment, and HPA suppression in children — short courses do not achieve remission and are not recommended.

data.stem: A 38-year-old woman with plaque psoriasis has failed adequate trials of topical corticosteroids and calcipotriol. Her PASI score is 13 and DLQI is 14. She asks about biologic therapy. Which response most accurately reflects the biologic referral criteria?
options: She does not qualify; biologic therapy requires failure of phototherapy before systemic or biologic agents
She qualifies; PASI >10 and DLQI >10 with failure of topical therapy meets standard biologic referral criteria
She qualifies only if she has psoriatic arthritis; skin-only disease cannot receive biologics
She qualifies; however, biologics cannot be started until methotrexate has been tried for at least 6 months
correctAnswer: She qualifies; PASI >10 and DLQI >10 with failure of topical therapy meets standard biologic referral criteria
data.rationale: PASI (Psoriasis Area and Severity Index) >10 and DLQI (Dermatology Life Quality Index) >10 together, with failure of adequate topical therapy, meet standard criteria for biologic referral. This patient meets both thresholds. PASI alone >10 or DLQI alone >10 with topical failure is sufficient in many guidelines. Option A is incorrect: while phototherapy is an option in the stepwise ladder, it is not mandatory before biologic referral in all guidelines; patients with PASI/DLQI scores meeting thresholds can be referred for biologic consideration after topical failure. Option C is incorrect: biologics (IL-17, IL-23, TNF-alpha inhibitors) are FDA-approved for moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis regardless of joint involvement. Option D is incorrect: methotrexate is an option in the stepwise ladder but is not a mandatory prerequisite for biologic referral in all cases — some guidelines allow biologic first after topical failure in patients who cannot tolerate or have contraindications to conventional systemics.

data.stem: A 14-year-old girl with severe atopic dermatitis presents to urgent care with a rapidly spreading rash. Over 24 hours, punched-out, monomorphic crusted erosions have spread from her usual AD distribution to her face, trunk, and arms. She has a fever of 38.6°C and appears ill. Her mother applied extra topical steroid cream last night. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Prescribe a medium-potency topical corticosteroid and oral antihistamine for acute AD flare
Diagnose eczema herpeticum; start systemic acyclovir and discontinue topical steroid
Start oral prednisone for severe atopic dermatitis flare with secondary infection
Prescribe oral antibiotic (cephalexin) for presumed staphylococcal superinfection of eczema
correctAnswer: Diagnose eczema herpeticum; start systemic acyclovir and discontinue topical steroid
data.rationale: Eczema herpeticum is HSV superinfection of atopic dermatitis — a dermatological emergency. The hallmark features are: punched-out, monomorphic (all the same size and stage) crusted vesicular erosions, rapid spread beyond typical AD distribution, fever, and systemic illness. Topical corticosteroids applied to HSV eczema herpeticum suppress local immunity and can cause catastrophic HSV dissemination. Acyclovir 400mg 5x/day (or valacyclovir 1g TID) must be started immediately, and topical steroids must be discontinued. Hospitalizing for IV acyclovir should be considered for widespread disease. Option A is incorrect: topical corticosteroids will worsen HSV dissemination — this is dangerous and contraindicated in eczema herpeticum. Option C is incorrect: oral prednisone is immunosuppressive and would compound the iatrogenic immunosuppression already caused by the topical steroid application; it is absolutely contraindicated in active viral infection. Option D is incorrect: while staphylococcal superinfection (impetiginization) is common in AD, it presents with honey-crusted lesions, not monomorphic punched-out erosions — the clinical pattern here is diagnostic for HSV.

data.stem: A 35-year-old woman with moderate-to-severe psoriasis is started on methotrexate 15mg weekly by her dermatologist. Which medication should be prescribed concurrently to reduce the most common serious side effects of methotrexate?
options: Leucovorin 5mg weekly 24 hours after each methotrexate dose
Folic acid 1mg daily on non-methotrexate days
Calcium 1000mg daily to prevent osteoporosis
Pyridoxine 50mg daily to reduce peripheral neuropathy risk
correctAnswer: Folic acid 1mg daily on non-methotrexate days
data.rationale: Folic acid 1mg daily (typically on days not taking methotrexate, though some protocols allow all days) is standard practice with methotrexate to reduce the most common side effects: mucositis, hepatotoxicity, and cytopenias. Folate supplementation reduces these adverse effects without significantly compromising methotrexate's therapeutic efficacy. Option A is incorrect: leucovorin (folinic acid) is used for methotrexate rescue in oncology overdose situations — it actively rescues cells from methotrexate inhibition and would reduce psoriasis efficacy if given weekly alongside therapeutic doses. Option C is incorrect: calcium supplementation does not address methotrexate toxicity — it is relevant for corticosteroid-induced osteoporosis but not methotrexate management. Option D is incorrect: pyridoxine is used for isoniazid-induced peripheral neuropathy and chemotherapy regimens — it is not standard practice with low-dose weekly methotrexate for psoriasis.

data.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents for a well visit and smokes 1 pack per day. She states she is 'not ready to quit right now.' She is not interested in pharmacotherapy. Which response best represents evidence-based counseling for a precontemplation patient?
options: Document her refusal and plan to address it again in 1 year at her next annual visit
Use the 5R framework: explore personal Relevance, Risks, Rewards, Roadblocks, and repeat the conversation at every visit
Refer to a smoking cessation specialist; readiness counseling is outside the NP scope
Prescribe a nicotine patch without her agreement to avoid withdrawal-related harms
correctAnswer: Use the 5R framework: explore personal Relevance, Risks, Rewards, Roadblocks, and repeat the conversation at every visit
data.rationale: For patients not ready to quit, the 5R framework is the evidence-based counseling approach: Relevance (personal health and family context), Risks (health consequences specific to her), Rewards (benefits of quitting), Roadblocks (barriers to quitting), and Repetition (returning to the topic at every visit). Brief motivational counseling at every visit significantly increases quit rates over time. Option A is incorrect: addressing tobacco use only at annual visits misses the opportunity to plant a seed at every encounter; brief counseling at every visit is proven more effective than annual discussion. Option C is incorrect: brief motivational counseling for smoking cessation is within every primary care NP's scope — this is core preventive medicine. Option D is incorrect: prescribing any medication without the patient's agreement and informed consent is unethical and a boundary violation; furthermore, an unrestricted NRT patch in a full-time smoker without a quit plan would be misused.

data.stem: A 52-year-old man with a 30 pack-year history wants to quit smoking. He has a history of major depressive disorder, currently stable on sertraline 100mg. He asks whether he can use varenicline. How should you counsel him regarding its safety in patients with psychiatric history?
options: Varenicline is contraindicated in any patient with a history of depression or psychiatric illness
Varenicline can be used with caution; the EAGLES trial showed no significant increase in neuropsychiatric adverse events compared to placebo in patients with stable psychiatric conditions
Switch his sertraline to bupropion, which doubles as an antidepressant and smoking cessation aid
Recommend NRT only; all non-NRT cessation aids are unsafe in patients on SSRIs
correctAnswer: Varenicline can be used with caution; the EAGLES trial showed no significant increase in neuropsychiatric adverse events compared to placebo in patients with stable psychiatric conditions
data.rationale: The EAGLES trial (2016) definitively evaluated neuropsychiatric safety of varenicline and bupropion versus NRT and placebo in patients with and without stable psychiatric illness. The trial found no significant increase in serious neuropsychiatric adverse events in patients with stable psychiatric conditions. Varenicline remains the most effective cessation agent and can be used in patients with controlled psychiatric disorders, including stable depression, with appropriate monitoring. Option A is incorrect: this was the former FDA concern prior to the EAGLES trial — the black box warning for neuropsychiatric adverse events was subsequently removed after EAGLES demonstrated safety. Option C is incorrect: switching sertraline to bupropion for the dual purpose is complex and risks destabilizing well-controlled depression; combining bupropion (NDRI) with sertraline (SSRI) also increases serotonin activity; this is not the recommended approach. Option D is incorrect: NRT is safe in combination with SSRIs, but restricting cessation pharmacotherapy to NRT only for all psychiatric patients is overly restrictive and not evidence-based post-EAGLES.

data.stem: A 58-year-old woman with CKD stage 4 (CrCl 22 mL/min calculated by Cockcroft-Gault) and 35 pack-years of smoking wants to quit. She asks about varenicline. What is the most appropriate prescribing decision?
options: Prescribe standard varenicline 0.5mg daily → 1mg BID; renal function does not affect varenicline dosing
Prescribe varenicline with renal dose reduction: start 0.5mg daily, maximum 0.5mg BID for CrCl <30
Avoid varenicline entirely in CKD; use bupropion SR as a safer alternative
Avoid all pharmacotherapy; recommend cessation counseling only for patients with CrCl <30
correctAnswer: Prescribe varenicline with renal dose reduction: start 0.5mg daily, maximum 0.5mg BID for CrCl <30
data.rationale: Varenicline is renally cleared and requires dose adjustment when CrCl <30 mL/min: start at 0.5mg once daily, with a maximum dose of 0.5mg BID (never titrate to the standard 1mg BID in this population due to drug accumulation risk). This patient's CrCl by Cockcroft-Gault is 22 mL/min — below the 30 threshold. She can and should use varenicline for the highest-efficacy cessation strategy, with the renal-adjusted dose. Option A is incorrect: renal function significantly affects varenicline — the drug is approximately 90% renally cleared; standard dosing would lead to accumulation and increased adverse effect risk (nausea, neuropsychiatric). Option C is incorrect: bupropion is also renally cleared (to some extent) and carries its own risks (seizure threshold lowering); avoiding varenicline when the correct renal dose adjustment exists is unnecessarily restrictive. Option D is incorrect: patients with CKD benefit from smoking cessation at least as much as the general population (smoking accelerates CKD progression); withholding pharmacotherapy from a highly motivated patient with CKD is inappropriate.

data.stem: A 44-year-old man with a history of alcohol use disorder (currently sober 3 years) and epilepsy managed with levetiracetam wants to quit smoking. He asks about bupropion SR. Which response is most clinically appropriate?
options: Bupropion SR is safe in patients with epilepsy on stable anticonvulsants; proceed with standard dosing
Bupropion SR is contraindicated in patients with a seizure disorder; use varenicline instead
Bupropion SR is contraindicated due to alcohol history; NRT is the only safe option
Bupropion SR is safe in patients sober for 3 years; dose-dependent seizure risk does not apply to controlled epilepsy
correctAnswer: Bupropion SR is contraindicated in patients with a seizure disorder; use varenicline instead
data.rationale: Bupropion SR has a dose-dependent seizure risk and is specifically contraindicated in patients with a seizure disorder (epilepsy), regardless of current anticonvulsant control. Even on levetiracetam, the additional seizure threshold lowering from bupropion represents an unacceptable risk. Varenicline is the appropriate alternative — it does not affect seizure threshold and is the most effective cessation agent. Option A is incorrect: epilepsy is an absolute contraindication to bupropion SR regardless of anticonvulsant therapy — adding bupropion creates pharmacodynamic risk regardless of concurrent AED use. Option C is incorrect: the relevant contraindication here is epilepsy, not alcohol use history; past alcohol use disorder (sober 3 years) is not a contraindication to bupropion — the seizure risk from abrupt alcohol withdrawal (acute intoxication context) is the relevant alcohol concern, which does not apply to a patient 3 years sober. Option D is incorrect: dose-dependent seizure risk from bupropion applies to patients with epilepsy regardless of controlled status — 'controlled epilepsy' on AEDs does not remove the contraindication.

data.stem: A 28-year-old woman at 16 weeks gestation smokes 10 cigarettes daily and is highly motivated to quit. She is concerned about using any medications during pregnancy. What is the most accurate counseling and recommended approach?
options: Advise her to continue smoking at the same rate; stopping abruptly in mid-pregnancy may harm the fetus
Recommend behavioral counseling alone; all pharmacotherapy is absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy
Offer NRT (patch plus short-acting form) as the preferred pharmacotherapy; continued smoking causes significantly greater fetal harm than NRT nicotine exposure
Prescribe varenicline; it is the safest and most effective option for highly motivated pregnant patients
correctAnswer: Offer NRT (patch plus short-acting form) as the preferred pharmacotherapy; continued smoking causes significantly greater fetal harm than NRT nicotine exposure
data.rationale: Nicotine replacement therapy (NRT) is the preferred smoking cessation pharmacotherapy in pregnancy. The key counseling point: the toxins from cigarette combustion (carbon monoxide, 4,000+ chemicals) cause significantly more fetal harm — including IUGR, placental abruption, stillbirth, and SIDS — than NRT's nicotine alone. Combination NRT (patch for baseline control + gum or lozenge for cravings) is safe and effective. Avoid overnight extended-release patches in the 3rd trimester. Option A is incorrect: advising a motivated patient to continue smoking in pregnancy is harmful — the goal is complete cessation. Option B is incorrect: behavioral counseling alone is less effective than combined pharmacotherapy + counseling in pregnancy; NRT is recommended when counseling alone is insufficient. Option D is incorrect: varenicline has insufficient safety data in pregnancy — animal studies show adverse effects and there is no adequate human RCT data; it is not recommended for use in pregnancy; NRT has the most established safety profile.
